proposals.title: Subarray Manifold Decoupling for Robust Direction Finding with Coherent Sources
proposals.motivation: High-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation algorithms, such as MUSIC and ESPRIT, suffer severe performance degradation in the presence of coherent or highly correlated signals, a common scenario in multipath and smart jamming environments. While spatial smoothing techniques applied to uniform linear arrays can restore rank, they are inefficient for arbitrary array geometries and reduce effective aperture. This problem necessitates a geometry-agnostic, theoretically grounded solution that preserves estimation accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically partitioning an arbitrary array into subarrays and formulating the estimation problem on the coupled covariance manifold of these subarrays, it is possible to decouple the signal coherence and recover the full-rank structure required for high-resolution DOA estimation without significant aperture loss.
proposals.method: We will develop a manifold-based framework where the array is partitioned into overlapping subarrays based on a coupling analysis of their covariance structures. The joint covariance matrix of these subarrays will be modeled as a point on a product manifold. We will derive a manifold optimization procedure, potentially using Riemannian conjugate gradient methods, to find a 'decoupled' point that minimizes a coherence metric while preserving the underlying signal subspace geometry. This processed covariance will then feed into standard subspace-based DOA estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, geometry-flexible algorithm for coherent source processing that minimizes effective aperture loss compared to conventional smoothing. It will contribute a new theoretical link between array covariance manifolds, subarray coupling, and rank restoration, offering a principled alternative to ad-hoc decorrelation techniques.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying advanced manifold concepts from Friedlander's prior work on array covariance manifolds and degrees of freedom directly to the coherence problem via a structured subarray coupling optimization, rather than using fixed smoothing patterns or purely algebraic pre-processing.

proposals.title: Robust Fusion of Subarray DOA Estimates via Manifold-Aware Statistical Coupling and Covariance Matching
proposals.motivation: Large-aperture or distributed arrays are often partitioned into subarrays for decentralized processing, but optimal combination of their Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) estimates remains an open challenge. Pre-2026 work on array covariance manifolds and the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) provides a geometric foundation, yet lacks a robust statistical framework for fusing estimates under practical conditions of non-identical noise, subarray calibration mismatches, and coherent local errors.
proposals.hypothesis: The covariance manifold structure and the statistical coupling between subarrays can be exploited to derive a robust fusion rule that minimizes the overall mean-squared error of the combined DOA estimate, even when individual subarray estimates are biased or their error statistics are mismatched.
proposals.method: We will model the joint estimation error of all subarrays by extending the covariance manifold framework to characterize the coupled perturbation space. A robust fusion estimator will be formulated as a weighted combination, where the weighting matrix is derived by minimizing a manifold-aware cost function that matches the empirical subarray covariance to the theoretical structure under mismatches. This will involve solving a Riemannian optimization problem on the coupled manifold, potentially using tools from robust statistics (e.g., M-estimation) to downweight outliers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, statistically robust algorithm for DOA fusion that guarantees performance closer to the global CRB in mismatched scenarios. It will yield a concrete metric for the effective 'degrees of freedom' in a coupled subarray system and deliver a practical calibration strategy for distributed arrays based on the fusion residuals.
proposals.novelty: While subarray processing and fusion exist, a rigorous fusion framework grounded in the joint covariance manifold geometry and explicitly designed for robustness against model mismatches is novel. The approach uniquely couples the statistical error analysis from CRB studies with the geometric perspective of array manifolds.

proposals.title: Manifold-Aware Sparse Array Optimization for Minimum-Element Direction Finding
proposals.motivation: Sparse array design, such as thinned arrays or minimum redundancy linear arrays (MRLAs), is a classic method to reduce hardware cost and complexity in direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation by minimizing the number of physical antenna elements. However, traditional sparse optimization often focuses solely on the co-array structure to maximize degrees of freedom, neglecting the detailed geometry of the array covariance manifold which fundamentally governs estimation performance. A principled integration of sparse design with the intrinsic manifold geometry is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By directly coupling the mathematical structure of the array covariance manifold with combinatorial sparse array optimization, we can design arrays with a minimal number of elements that preserve the fast, high-resolution estimation properties of the manifold-based method, achieving near-Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRB) performance with significantly reduced hardware.
proposals.method: We will formulate a constrained optimization problem where the objective is to minimize the number of active elements subject to a performance constraint derived from the manifold's local curvature, as quantified by the CRB analysis from Friedlander's prior work. The search over sparse array configurations will be guided by the structure of the array manifold's Fisher information matrix. We will employ a hybrid algorithm combining greedy backward elimination (thinning) with local search, where at each candidate array geometry, we rapidly evaluate the expected CRB using the fast manifold-based method, avoiding full Monte Carlo simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new design framework for cost-effective sensor arrays that are optimal from an estimation-theoretic perspective, not just a co-array perspective. It will yield concrete, publishable sparse array layouts for common DOA scenarios and establish a theoretical link between array sparsity, manifold geometry, and the fundamental limits of estimation accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of combinatorial array thinning with the differential geometric properties of the covariance manifold, moving beyond the virtual array paradigm to optimize the actual physical array for estimation performance on the manifold itself.

proposals.title: Sparse Fusion of Subarray Covariance Manifolds for Super-Resolution Direction Finding
proposals.motivation: Large-aperture arrays are often partitioned into subarrays for decentralized processing, but fusing their independent direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimates is challenging due to ambiguous grating lobes and inconsistent calibrations. Existing fusion methods, like voting or likelihood combination, fail to exploit the underlying joint sparsity of the source scene across the full array, limiting resolution and robustness. A principled, geometry-aware fusion framework is needed to unify subarray data into a coherent, high-performance estimate.
proposals.hypothesis: The manifold structure of the full array's covariance matrix can be recovered from subarray outputs by solving a joint sparse recovery problem, where the common angular support is enforced as a constraint, leading to a unified DOA estimate that surpasses the performance of any individual subarray.
proposals.method: We will formulate the subarray covariance matrices as projections of a single, full-array sparse spatial spectrum. Using the array manifold geometry, we construct an overcomplete dictionary for the full aperture. A group-sparse optimization problem (e.g., using the group LASSO or sparse Bayesian learning) will be solved to recover the common support from the subarray covariance vectors or their principal eigenvectors. The method will explicitly account for subarray-specific calibration errors by incorporating them as structured uncertainties within the optimization framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous compressive sensing framework for subarray data fusion, directly linking array manifold theory to sparse recovery. It will yield a practical algorithm that improves DOA estimation accuracy and resolution for partitioned arrays, with explicit bounds on performance derived from the Cramér-Rao analysis of the fused estimator.
proposals.novelty: While compressive sensing for DOA is well-studied, its application to fuse *already processed* subarray estimates by exploiting the covariance manifold structure is novel. This approach moves beyond simple data concatenation to a model-based fusion that respects the geometric constraints of the full array.

proposals.title: Covariance Manifold Analysis and Cramér-Rao Bounds for Direction-of-Arrival Estimation in Massive MIMO with Distributed Coprime Subarrays
proposals.motivation: Massive MIMO systems promise unprecedented spectral efficiency and resolution, but their scale introduces prohibitive hardware complexity, channel estimation overhead, and computational cost for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation. Pre-2026 research on coprime arrays and manifold geometry, such as Friedlander's work on degrees-of-freedom and covariance manifolds, offers a path to reduce these burdens by exploiting sparse array structures, but these concepts have not been rigorously extended to distributed, very large-scale deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system of distributed, spatially separated coprime subarrays can achieve near-optimal DOA estimation performance of a fully populated massive array, while dramatically reducing hardware and computational costs, and that the fundamental performance limits can be characterized by analyzing the coupled covariance manifold of the distributed system.
proposals.method: We will develop a mathematical framework modeling the distributed coprime subarray system as a coupled set of manifolds. The core method involves deriving the structured Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) for this architecture by extending Friedlander's data-based CRB estimation techniques to the distributed, sparse case. We will design calibration and coupling algorithms to synchronize the subarrays and fuse their covariance data, then implement and test high-resolution DOA estimators (e.g., sparse recovery or manifold-based MUSIC variants) that exploit the combined aperture and reduced dimensionality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretical performance bound and a practical system architecture for scalable massive MIMO DOA estimation. It will deliver concrete algorithms for calibration and estimation in distributed coprime arrays, validated by simulation, demonstrating a viable path to deploy very large-scale arrays with manageable complexity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of coprime array geometry with a distributed, multi-subarray architecture for massive MIMO, coupled with a rigorous performance analysis grounded in covariance manifold theory and CRB derivation specific to this sparse, distributed structure.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Coprime Arrays: A Manifold-Based Framework for Specifying DOA Accuracy and Complexity
proposals.motivation: Coprime sensor arrays are a key architecture for achieving high degrees-of-freedom with fewer physical elements, but their design remains largely ad-hoc. Current practice selects coprime integers M and N based on heuristic rules or trial-and-error to meet a target aperture or number of unique lags, without a direct, principled link to the ultimate performance metric: Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) estimation accuracy. A systematic inverse design methodology is needed to translate a desired Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) performance into optimal array parameters, closing the loop between theory and practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: The covariance manifold structure of a coprime array, characterized by the integers (M, N), dictates a fundamental performance-complexity trade-off; therefore, for a given target DOA accuracy (CRB) and computational budget, there exists a unique or Pareto-optimal pair (M, N) that can be determined analytically.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem by deriving a closed-form, approximate mapping from (M, N) to the CRB for a canonical source scenario, leveraging Friedlander's prior work on the CRB and array manifold. This mapping will be embedded in a constrained optimization framework where the objective is to minimize the number of physical sensors (M+N-1) subject to a CRB inequality constraint. The search over the integer space will employ number-theoretic properties of coprime pairs and utilize branch-and-bound algorithms to ensure global optimality. Complexity will be quantified via the coupling matrix structure and the ensuing computational load of subspace methods like MUSIC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous methodology for the inverse design of coprime arrays, yielding explicit design charts linking accuracy specifications to optimal (M, N). It will advance array theory by formalizing the performance-complexity trade-off on the covariance manifold. Practically, it will deliver a tool for system engineers to rapidly synthesize optimal array configurations for given mission requirements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes performance for a given array, we invert this relationship to *synthesize* the array from performance specs, a non-trivial problem due to the discrete, non-linear nature of the parameter space. The novelty lies in coupling the CRB-based accuracy metric directly to the number-theoretic array parameters within an optimization loop.

proposals.title: Inverse Array Design: Optimizing Sensor Manifold Geometry for Shaped ESPRIT via Covariance Manifold Analysis
proposals.motivation: Traditional array design is often heuristic or based on generic criteria like uniform angular resolution. For high-resolution subspace methods like ESPRIT, performance is fundamentally linked to the structure of the array manifold and its covariance. The 'Shaped ESPRIT' framework, which exploits specific invariances, lacks a principled method to determine the optimal physical sensor arrangement that maximizes its estimation accuracy for a given signal environment.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal sensor geometry for Shaped ESPRIT, under a specific signal and noise model, can be derived as the solution to an inverse problem on the array covariance manifold, directly minimizing the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) for direction-of-arrival estimation.
proposals.method: We will formulate the design as a constrained optimization on the manifold of array covariance matrices. The objective function will be a scalar measure (e.g., trace or maximum eigenvalue) of the CRB, expressed analytically in terms of the array manifold vectors. Using tools from differential geometry on manifolds of structured matrices, we will derive gradients with respect to sensor coordinates. Numerical optimization (e.g., manifold-based gradient descent or sequential convex programming) will be employed to solve for the optimal sensor positions, explicitly enforcing the displacement invariances required for Shaped ESPRIT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, model-based framework for designing purpose-built arrays that are optimal for a specific high-resolution algorithm (Shaped ESPRIT) and operational scenario. It will bridge the gap between statistical estimation theory (CRB) and physical array geometry, offering a concrete design methodology beyond rule-of-thumb layouts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes performance for given arrays, this inverts the problem: starting from an algorithm's (Shaped ESPRIT) mathematical structure and a performance bound (CRB), it synthesizes the optimal physical array shape, treating sensor positions as continuous optimization variables on a constrained manifold.

proposals.title: Quantization-Aware Weighted Alternating Least Squares for Scalable Massive MIMO Array Calibration
proposals.motivation: Massive MIMO systems with hundreds or thousands of antennas are central to 5G-Advanced and future 6G networks, but their performance critically depends on accurate array calibration. Traditional high-precision calibration algorithms, like Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS), become computationally and energetically prohibitive at this scale, creating a bottleneck for practical deployment. This work addresses the need for scalable, energy-efficient calibration methods that can operate within the constraints of real-time baseband processing hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically integrating low-precision, quantized numerical representations (e.g., INT8, FP16) into the WALS optimization loop with quantization-aware mechanisms, we can achieve a massive reduction in computational complexity and memory bandwidth for calibration, while maintaining estimation accuracy and robustness comparable to full-precision methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a Quantization-Aware WALS (QA-WALS) algorithm where all matrix operations (e.g., weighted least-squares solves, covariance calculations) are performed in low precision. The core innovation is the integration of gradient scaling and stochastic rounding techniques, inspired by quantization-aware training in deep learning (pre-2026), directly into the alternating optimization steps to mitigate bias from quantization noise. We will analyze the algorithm's convergence and derive bounds on the resulting calibration error relative to the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound, leveraging Friedlander's prior work on estimation bounds and array manifold structure.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, hardware-friendly calibration algorithm that enables scalable massive MIMO deployment. It will provide a rigorous theoretical and empirical framework for understanding the trade-offs between numerical precision, computational cost, and calibration accuracy in large arrays. The resulting software-defined calibration core will be a key enabler for energy-efficient next-generation base stations.
proposals.novelty: While low-precision computation is known in deep learning and some signal processing domains, its principled application to the structured, model-based optimization of array calibration—specifically the WALS framework—is novel. The fusion of quantization-aware training principles with the geometric constraints of the array covariance manifold represents a significant advance over simple, post-hoc quantization of existing algorithms.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Degradation of the Cramér-Rao Bound for Direction-of-Arrival Estimation in Coprime Arrays Under Mutual Coupling
proposals.motivation: Coprime arrays (CRAs) are prized for their ability to achieve high degrees of freedom and superior resolution from a limited number of physical sensors. However, their performance guarantees, often quantified by the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), typically assume ideal, uncoupled antennas. In practice, mutual coupling between non-uniformly spaced elements in a CRA distorts the array manifold and signal covariance structure, leading to unquantified performance loss. A rigorous, model-based analysis of this degradation is absent in the literature, creating a gap between theoretical promise and practical implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mutual coupling in coprime arrays introduces a structured perturbation to the array manifold and the signal covariance matrix, leading to a quantifiable increase in the deterministic CRB for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation that depends on the coupling matrix structure and the CRA geometry.
proposals.method: We will derive a closed-form expression for the perturbed CRB by incorporating a full mutual coupling matrix, modeled via the coupling formulation from pre-2026 literature (e.g., based on antenna scattering parameters), into the signal model. The analysis will leverage manifold geometry concepts to characterize the distortion. We will validate the theoretical bound through numerical simulations using high-fidelity electromagnetic models (e.g., Method of Moments) for specific CRA geometries and compare it against the performance of practical estimators like MUSIC and sparse recovery methods under the same coupling conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first analytical framework for predicting the fundamental performance limits of DOA estimation in coupled coprime arrays. It will yield practical design guidelines for CRA element spacing and subarray configuration to mitigate coupling effects. The results will directly inform calibration requirements and the validity of uncoupled performance predictions in real-world systems.
proposals.novelty: While mutual coupling's general impact is known, a rigorous CRB analysis specific to the non-uniform geometry of coprime arrays, linking coupling to the manifold structure and degrees-of-freedom framework, is novel. This moves beyond generic sensitivity analysis to a geometry-aware fundamental limit.

proposals.title: Mutual Coupling Mitigation in Sparse Arrays via Integrated Decoupling Networks: A Manifold and Cramér-Rao Bound Analysis
proposals.motivation: Sparse arrays offer high degrees-of-freedom for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation but suffer from severe performance degradation due to mutual coupling, which distorts the array manifold and covariance structure. Pre-2026 calibration and modeling techniques are often data-driven and post-hoc, failing to address the fundamental electromagnetic coupling at the hardware level, limiting the practical utility of sparse geometries.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating circuit-based decoupling networks, adapted from RF front-end design, into the sparse array architecture will fundamentally reshape the effective array manifold and covariance matrix, leading to quantifiable improvements in parameter estimation bounds and practical DOA estimation performance.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate decoupling networks (e.g., using lumped-element matching circuits or transmission-line networks) for canonical sparse arrays (e.g., nested, coprime). The modified electromagnetic response will be used to derive the new, physically-realistic array manifold and covariance model. We will then analyze the impact on the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) for DOA estimation, extending Friedlander's prior CRB work, and validate the models with numerical simulations of standard algorithms (e.g., MUSIC) under coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, hardware-aware framework for sparse array design that explicitly controls mutual coupling. It will yield new, physically accurate models for the array manifold and covariance structure of decoupled sparse arrays and establish new theoretical performance bounds for such systems.
proposals.novelty: While decoupling networks are known in antenna design, their systematic integration and analysis for enhancing the *estimation-theoretic performance* of sparse arrays—bridging circuit theory, electromagnetic modeling, and statistical signal processing—is a novel, cross-disciplinary approach not explored in pre-2026 array processing literature.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Optimization of Sparse Array Geometries for Enhanced Degrees of Freedom and Estimation Performance
proposals.motivation: Optimal sparse array design is a high-dimensional, non-convex combinatorial problem critical for maximizing degrees of freedom (DOF) and minimizing estimation error bounds like the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) in direction-finding. Exhaustive search is intractable for large arrays, and existing analytical or greedy methods often yield suboptimal configurations or are limited to specific metrics. An efficient, flexible search strategy is needed to explore the vast geometry space for complex, multi-objective design criteria.
proposals.hypothesis: A genetic algorithm (GA) framework, tailored to the manifold structure of array covariance matrices, can efficiently discover sparse array configurations that outperform known structured arrays (e.g., nested, coprime) in terms of achievable DOF and CRB for a given number of physical sensors.
proposals.method: We will formulate the array element positions as a binary chromosome. The fitness function will integrate key manifold-based metrics from Friedlander's prior work: the achievable DOF (linked to the covariance manifold's rank properties) and a data-based CRB estimator for direction-of-arrival. A custom GA will be implemented with specialized crossover and mutation operators that respect physical array constraints (e.g., minimum spacing). The search will be benchmarked against canonical sparse arrays and optimized using a coupling between the GA's population dynamics and the mathematical structure of the array manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a powerful, general-purpose computational tool for array design that directly optimizes for estimation-theoretic performance. It will yield new, non-intuitive array geometries that push the limits of sparse sensing. The results will also offer empirical insights into the relationship between array geometry, the covariance manifold, and fundamental estimation bounds.
proposals.novelty: While GAs have been applied to array synthesis, their use is novel when directly driven by manifold-derived degrees-of-freedom and a data-based CRB estimator, creating a direct link between evolutionary search and the core mathematical structure governing estimation performance.

proposals.title: Sparse Array Synthesis for Maximum Inter-Element Coupling: A Manifold Optimization Framework for Wireless Power Transfer
proposals.motivation: In array processing, especially for direction-of-arrival estimation, the dominant design paradigm is to minimize mutual coupling to preserve signal orthogonality and simplify calibration. However, emerging applications like near-field wireless power transfer (WPT) and secure communications fundamentally require strong, controlled coupling between array elements. Pre-2026, sparse array design has been optimized for degrees-of-freedom and spatial resolution, but not for maximizing and shaping the coupling matrix for energy transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: The covariance manifold of an array, which encodes its spatial degrees-of-freedom, can be reformulated to characterize the manifold of achievable coupling matrices, enabling the systematic design of sparse arrays that maximize total transferred power under practical constraints.
proposals.method: We will formulate the coupling matrix as a function of array geometry and near-field electromagnetic models. Using the mathematical framework of array manifolds and covariance matrices from Friedlander's prior work, we will define a cost function (e.g., the Frobenius norm of the coupling matrix) to maximize. The core technical approach will involve developing a manifold optimization algorithm, potentially using Riemannian conjugate gradient methods on the space of sparse array geometries, to solve this non-convex synthesis problem. We will incorporate constraints on total aperture, number of elements, and minimum element spacing to ensure physical realizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled methodology for designing sparse arrays explicitly for maximum coupling, establishing a new application for array manifold theory. It will deliver concrete array geometries and a computational design tool for WPT systems, potentially increasing their efficiency and range. The analysis will also yield fundamental bounds on maximum achievable coupling for a given number of elements and aperture.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the classic array design objective from minimizing to maximizing coupling and applies the sophisticated tools of covariance manifold analysis—previously used for estimation-theoretic performance—to the fundamentally different problem of near-field energy transfer.

proposals.title: Cramér-Rao Bounds and Manifold Geometry for Direction Finding with Incoherent, Random-Phase Signals
proposals.motivation: Standard direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation theory and algorithms, such as MUSIC and ESPRIT, rely on coherent signal models with stable phase relationships across the array. However, in practical scenarios involving rapidly fluctuating sources, distributed scatterers, or certain communication waveforms, signals exhibit random, unknown phase components that are not captured by these models. This incoherence fundamentally alters the signal covariance structure and the underlying estimation manifold, yet a rigorous performance analysis and associated estimation framework are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling signals with random, unknown phases will lead to a distinct covariance manifold geometry, resulting in modified Cramér-Rao bounds (CRBs) and a reduction in effective degrees of freedom for the array, necessitating new estimation strategies that explicitly account for this phase uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will first derive the exact data covariance matrix for an array receiving multiple narrowband signals with i.i.d. random phase components per snapshot. This model will be used to characterize the resulting covariance manifold, extending Friedlander's prior work on manifold geometry. We will then compute the deterministic CRB for DOA estimation under this model, analyzing its dependence on phase distribution and SNR. Finally, we will develop a tailored estimation algorithm, likely based on a covariance matching or stochastic maximum-likelihood approach, that incorporates the phase uncertainty into the optimization framework, contrasting its performance with standard coherent estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive performance bounds for DOA estimation under a generalized incoherent signal model, clarifying the theoretical cost of phase uncertainty. It will yield a new, practical estimation algorithm robust to such signal characteristics and offer deeper insight into the coupling between signal model assumptions and array manifold properties.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats phase as either fully coherent or fully random (decorrelated), our model introduces a structured yet unknown phase, creating a novel hybrid manifold. The explicit integration of this per-snapshot random phase into the CRB derivation and estimator design for general array geometries is a non-trivial extension of existing array processing theory.

proposals.title: Robust Subspace Estimation for Array Processing via M-Estimators on the Covariance Manifold
proposals.motivation: High-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation relies on accurate knowledge of the array manifold and its covariance structure. In practice, manifold errors from calibration imperfections, near-field effects, or mutual coupling degrade subspace-based methods like MUSIC. Pre-2026 robust techniques often impose bounded uncertainty sets or worst-case optimization, which can be overly conservative and lack a statistical foundation for typical error distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling steering vector uncertainties as statistical outliers and applying robust M-estimation principles directly on the covariance matrix manifold will yield more efficient and statistically stable subspace estimates than conventional worst-case or diagonal loading approaches.
proposals.method: The method will formulate the sample covariance matrix as a point on the Hermitian positive-definite manifold. We will develop a novel cost function using a robust loss (e.g., Huber, Tukey) applied to the residuals between the measured covariance and a low-rank plus identity model, penalizing deviations from the assumed signal subspace structure. Optimization will be performed via iterative reweighted geodesic steps on the manifold, coupling Riemannian geometry with robust statistics. The resulting robust covariance estimate will then feed into standard subspace decomposition for DOA estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically principled framework for robust subspace estimation that naturally accounts for the geometry of covariance matrices. It will deliver a new algorithm with predictable breakdown points and established asymptotic efficiency, bridging the gap between robust statistics and array signal processing. The analysis will also offer new insights into the effective degrees of freedom under model misspecification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of robust M-estimation theory with explicit optimization on the covariance manifold, a departure from prior ad-hoc diagonal loading or bounded error models. This provides a direct, statistically rigorous mechanism to mitigate subspace errors without resorting to convex approximations or uncertainty set projections.

proposals.title: Bayesian Detection and Estimation on the Array Manifold with Uncertain Sensor Coordinates
proposals.motivation: Array processing algorithms for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation and source detection fundamentally rely on precise knowledge of the array manifold, which is often compromised by imperfect sensor calibration, position errors, or environmental deformation. Pre-2026 deterministic error bounds, like the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB), provide performance limits but do not offer a direct framework for incorporating prior structural knowledge about these uncertainties into the detection/estimation process itself.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating a Bayesian detector with explicit priors on unknown sensor coordinates will yield superior detection probability and more robust parameter estimation in low-SNR regimes compared to methods that treat array errors as deterministic unknowns or ignore them.
proposals.method: We will model the unknown sensor coordinates as random variables with a prior distribution (e.g., Gaussian, uniform within bounds) defined on the manifold of possible array configurations. The likelihood function will be derived from the received signal model, coupling the source parameters and the uncertain array geometry. Inference will be performed via a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler, such as a Gibbs or Metropolis-Hastings algorithm, to approximate the joint posterior distribution of source presence, DOAs, and sensor coordinates. Detection will be based on the Bayes factor computed from the model evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled statistical framework for joint detection, source localization, and array self-calibration under uncertainty. It will extend the analysis of array degrees-of-freedom and covariance manifolds to the Bayesian setting, offering practical algorithms with quantifiable uncertainty in the estimates of both source and array parameters.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods exist for DOA estimation, the explicit integration of a probabilistic prior on the sensor coordinates into a unified detection and estimation framework is novel. This moves beyond deterministic perturbation models and CRLB analyses to a full Bayesian treatment of the array manifold's uncertainty.

proposals.title: Manifold Geometry and Performance Bounds for Ultra-Low-Rank Signal Models in Array Processing
proposals.motivation: Modern array processing increasingly deals with signals constrained to very low-dimensional subspaces, such as in massive MIMO with limited scattering or in radar with few targets. Pre-2026 analysis of estimation bounds and array degrees of freedom often assumes higher-rank models, potentially obscuring fundamental limits and calibration requirements in these sparse regimes. A dedicated framework is needed to understand the geometry and intrinsic estimation constraints of ultra-low-rank signal models.
proposals.hypothesis: The covariance manifold of an array, when the signal subspace dimension is much smaller than the number of antennas, exhibits a simplified geometric structure that leads to closed-form, data-amenable Cramér-Rao bounds and clarifies the minimal calibration requirements for unambiguous estimation.
proposals.method: We will extend the manifold framework from Friedlander's 'Degrees-of-Freedom and the Covariance Manifold' work to the asymptotic regime where the signal rank is fixed as the array size grows. Using tools from Riemannian geometry on low-rank matrix manifolds, we will derive the intrinsic dimensionality and curvature. This geometric analysis will be coupled with statistical methods from Friedlander's data-based CRB work to develop new, scalable bounds for Direction-of-Arrival estimation, specifically testing them against subspace-based algorithms like MUSIC and ESPRIT under ultra-low-rank conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous geometric-statistical theory for performance limits in sparse signal environments, directly applicable to system design in massive MIMO and next-generation radar. It will yield new, computable bounds that inform the necessary array calibration complexity and the fundamental trade-off between array size and estimation accuracy when the signal dimension is minimal.
proposals.novelty: While low-rank models are well-studied, a dedicated geometric analysis of the *ultra-low-rank* asymptotic regime for array manifolds, coupled with practical, data-driven bound estimation, is unexplored. This bridges Friedlander's prior work on manifold geometry with his work on empirical bounds, targeting a specific and increasingly relevant scaling limit.

proposals.title: Resolving the Spectral-Structure Degeneracy in Wideband Interferometry via Constrained Manifold Optimization
proposals.motivation: Wideband interferometric calibration is fundamentally ill-posed due to the degeneracy between the unknown spectral structure of celestial sources and the frequency-dependent instrumental response. This degeneracy corrupts recovered source spectra and limits the dynamic range and fidelity of wideband images, a critical problem for modern instruments like the VLA and MeerKAT. Existing calibration methods often assume a simple sky model or treat gains per channel, failing to incorporate the separable physical constraints on the source and gain manifolds.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the spectral structure of the sky (via spectral index maps) and the instrumental gains (via smoothness in frequency) as separable components on distinct manifolds, and solving for them jointly with appropriate regularization, we can break the degeneracy and achieve robust, physically meaningful wideband calibration.
proposals.method: We will formulate calibration as a constrained optimization on a product manifold: the antenna gain manifold (enforcing smoothness via low-order polynomials or splines in frequency) and the sky model manifold (parameterized by per-source or per-pixel spectral indices). The cost function will be the data fidelity term (e.g., negative log-likelihood) plus regularization terms penalizing deviation from spectral smoothness for gains and sparsity in spectral index variations. We will develop a solver based on Riemannian optimization techniques (e.g., Manopt) to efficiently handle the manifold constraints and the non-convex joint estimation problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous mathematical framework and a practical algorithm for degeneracy-aware wideband calibration. It will directly improve the accuracy of recovered source spectra and wideband sky images for radio astronomical surveys. The method will also yield more reliable estimates of instrumental parameters, aiding in array characterization and data quality assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, simultaneous separation and estimation of the two degenerate spectral components on their respective constrained manifolds within a single optimization, moving beyond sequential or per-channel approaches common in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Joint Array Calibration and Source Estimation via Coupling-Aware Network Tomography
proposals.motivation: Accurate array calibration is critical for high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, but mutual coupling between antenna elements remains a significant source of performance degradation. Traditional methods often treat coupling as a nuisance to be measured in isolation or de-embedded, which is impractical in dynamic field deployments. A framework that jointly infers coupling and source parameters from operational array data is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The electromagnetic coupling network of a sensor array can be treated as a graph with unknown link states, and the joint problem of estimating this network and the external source scene is analogous to, and can be solved by, network tomography techniques that infer internal parameters from end-to-end path measurements.
proposals.method: We will formulate the array signal model as a linear system where the received signal vector is a function of the source scene and a structured, sparse coupling matrix. This inverse problem will be cast as a constrained optimization, leveraging pre-2026 network tomography algorithms (e.g., based on linear inverse problems with sparsity regularization, or expectation-maximization). We will exploit the physical structure of the coupling manifold—typically banded or decaying with distance—as prior constraints (e.g., via group Lasso or Toeplitz regularization) to ensure identifiability and develop efficient solvers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic framework for self-calibrating arrays using operational signals, eliminating the need for isolated calibration sessions. It will yield a more accurate characterization of the array's covariance manifold under mutual coupling, directly improving fundamental bounds like the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for DOA estimation in coupled arrays.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual transfer of network tomography to array signal processing, actively using coupling signals for system identification rather than merely canceling them. This joint inference approach contrasts with sequential isolation/de-embedding methods prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Unified Probabilistic Framework for Robust Array Calibration with Unknown Source Correlations
proposals.motivation: Array calibration in radio astronomy and other fields often assumes calibrator sources are perfectly uncorrelated. This assumption is frequently violated in practice due to source clustering or diffuse emission, leading to biased gain estimates and degraded imaging. Existing self-calibration methods either ignore source correlations or require their structure to be known a priori, creating a significant robustness gap for modern high-dynamic-range instruments.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly estimating the array calibration parameters and the unknown source correlation matrix within a regularized probabilistic model, it is possible to achieve robust, unbiased calibration without prior knowledge of the source structure, while simultaneously quantifying uncertainties in both the gains and the inferred correlations.
proposals.method: We will formulate a hierarchical Bayesian model where the observed visibilities depend on the complex gains and the source covariance matrix. The unknown correlation matrix will be parameterized via a low-rank plus sparse decomposition to separate physical source structure from systematic errors. Inference will be performed using a variational expectation-maximization (VEM) algorithm, leveraging manifold optimization for the gain parameters on the complex circle group and employing L1 and nuclear norm regularization for the correlation matrix to ensure identifiability and prevent overfitting.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical self-calibration algorithm robust to partially correlated calibrator fields, directly addressing a key limitation in radio interferometric calibration. It will provide a principled framework for uncertainty quantification in both the calibration solutions and the inferred sky structure, enhancing the reliability of downstream scientific analysis.
proposals.novelty: The joint, regularized estimation of the full source correlation matrix alongside the gains is a significant departure from methods that treat correlations as a nuisance or require a known model. The explicit decomposition and model selection within the correlation matrix to distinguish physics from systematics is a novel extension of manifold-based array processing.

proposals.title: Joint Gain and Correlation Structure Estimation for Robust Radio Interferometric Calibration
proposals.motivation: Radio interferometric calibration fundamentally assumes calibrator sources are fully uncorrelated. This is violated for complex, extended, or partially coherent fields, such as galactic supernova remnants or AGN lobes, leading to biased gain solutions and corrupted science data. Existing methods treat this as an unwanted nuisance, lacking a principled framework to model and estimate the partial correlation structure inherent in realistic calibrator emission.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the partial correlation between calibrator signals within a blind source separation framework, it is possible to jointly and more accurately estimate antenna gains and the source covariance structure, thereby improving calibration fidelity for complex fields.
proposals.method: We will adapt correlation-aware Independent Component Analysis (ICA) techniques, analogous to those in computational neuroscience for dependent sources. The core is a regularized maximum likelihood formulation where the data model is the standard radio interferometric measurement equation, but the source covariance matrix is parameterized with a low-rank plus sparse structure to capture partial correlations. We will develop an alternating minimization algorithm, iteratively updating the complex antenna gains (manifold optimization on the array covariance manifold) and the structured source covariance, using Lagrange multipliers or proximal operators to enforce the correlation constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new algorithmic framework for robust calibration in the presence of partially correlated calibrators, directly extending the theory of array covariance manifolds. It will yield a practical tool for next-generation telescopes observing complex fields, with a derived Cramér-Rao bound analysis to quantify the theoretical improvement in gain estimation accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally importing and adapting 'dependent source' ICA concepts from neuroscience to radio interferometry and embedding them within a rigorous array signal processing and manifold optimization framework for joint correlation-gain estimation, a departure from the universal independence assumption.

proposals.title: Array Calibration on the Covariance Manifold Under Signal Coherence: A Coupling and Subspace Projection Approach
proposals.motivation: Array calibration algorithms typically assume calibrator signals are uncorrelated, a condition often violated in practice due to multipath or intentional coherent sources. This mismatch degrades the accuracy of direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation and other array processing tasks. Addressing this limitation is crucial for robust performance in real-world environments like urban radar or indoor wireless systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the signal correlation structure and its interaction with the array covariance manifold, a modified calibration algorithm can be developed that is robust to partial or full coherence among calibrator sources, preserving estimation performance.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage the geometric structure of the covariance manifold, as studied in Friedlander's prior work. We will formulate a coupled optimization problem that jointly estimates the array response (calibration parameters) and the signal correlation matrix. Techniques from subspace separation, such as oblique projection operators and low-rank matrix completion, will be integrated to disentangle the correlated signal subspace from the array perturbation effects. The Cramér-Rao Bound analysis will be adapted to this correlated scenario to benchmark performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical calibration framework for scenarios with correlated sources, extending the utility of existing array processing algorithms. It will yield new theoretical insights into the geometry of the covariance manifold under signal coherence and deliver a concrete algorithm with provable robustness properties.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard calibration methods that ignore or pre-whiten correlation, this proposal directly incorporates the correlation structure into the manifold geometry and calibration cost function. The coupling of array parameter estimation with structured correlation matrix estimation represents a significant departure from conventional sequential processing.

proposals.title: Generalized Weighted Alternating Least Squares for Direction-of-Arrival Estimation with Heterogeneous Array Elements
proposals.motivation: Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) estimation algorithms, including the Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) method, typically assume identical, omnidirectional sensor elements. In practice, arrays often comprise sensors with different, known directional gain patterns (e.g., mixed antenna types, conformal arrays). This mismatch between model and reality introduces unmodeled errors, degrading estimation accuracy and invalidating standard performance bounds like the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) derived for isotropic elements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly incorporating individual sensor gain patterns into the signal model and the WALS optimization framework, we can formulate a generalized WALS algorithm that achieves statistically efficient DOA estimation for heterogeneous arrays, with a corresponding accurate data-based CRLB estimator.
proposals.method: We will extend the WALS framework by modeling the array manifold as a function of both source direction and individual sensor gain patterns, creating a higher-dimensional parameterized covariance manifold. The algorithm will alternate between estimating source parameters (DOA, power) and structured covariance fitting, with weights derived from the inverse of the estimated covariance. The core challenge is the efficient coupling of the non-identical element responses within the least-squares projection steps. We will derive the new Fisher Information Matrix and a data-based estimator for the CRLB specific to this heterogeneous model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, computationally tractable DOA estimation method for arrays with non-identical elements, closing a gap between standard theory and practical deployment. It will also deliver a tool for predicting fundamental estimation limits in such arrays, extending the concept of array degrees-of-freedom to account for element heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: While element calibration is studied, a systematic generalization of the WALS optimization to intrinsically heterogeneous array manifolds—and the derivation of its associated performance bounds—is novel. This moves beyond simple post-calibration correction to a fundamental reformulation of the estimation problem.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Weighted Subspace Fitting Methods Using Geophysical Array Calibration Paradigms
proposals.motivation: Weighted Subspace Fitting (WSF) and Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) are theoretically powerful for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, but their performance is often analyzed via simulation under idealized conditions. A critical gap exists in understanding their practical behavior with real sensor data, where manifold mismatches, calibration errors, and complex noise dominate. Borrowing the rigorous validation framework from calibrated seismic arrays in geophysics provides a principled path to bridge this theory-practice divide.
proposals.hypothesis: That WSF and WALS algorithms, when applied to experimental data from a physically calibrated sensor array, will demonstrate superior robustness to calibration errors and non-ideal array geometries compared to standard methods, while closely approaching their theoretical performance bounds derived from the array's covariance manifold.
proposals.method: We will source experimental datasets from existing, well-characterized array installations (e.g., acoustic source localization testbeds or RF antenna ranges) where ground-truth source locations are known. The core technical approach involves applying the WSF and WALS estimators to this data, explicitly modeling the array manifold using the physical sensor positions and incorporating estimated coupling matrices. Performance will be benchmarked against ground truth and classical methods (e.g., MUSIC, ML) by evaluating RMSE, probability of resolution, and bias. A key analysis will involve intentionally introducing known perturbations to the calibration parameters to systematically test algorithm robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive experimental performance characterization of WSF/WALS methods against physical ground truth, establishing their practical limits and failure modes. It will yield a validated framework for transitioning advanced array processing theory to engineered systems, directly informing design choices for future sensor networks in communications, radar, and acoustics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary adaptation of the geophysical array validation paradigm—using independently verified physical events—to the core signal processing problem of subspace-based DOA estimation, moving beyond synthetic simulations to a rigorous, data-driven assessment of these algorithms.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Weighted Subspace Fitting Methods on Physical Sensor Arrays: Performance, Calibration, and Manifold Mismatch
proposals.motivation: Weighted Subspace Fitting (WSF) and Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) methods are theoretically optimal for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation under ideal conditions, but their performance on real-world arrays is poorly characterized. Pre-2026 literature lacks comprehensive studies using experimental data to validate these methods against practical challenges like mutual coupling, imperfect calibration, and manifold errors, which are critical for applications in radar, sonar, and wireless communications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance degradation of WSF and WALS methods on experimental data can be accurately predicted and mitigated by explicitly modeling the dominant error mechanisms—specifically, residual calibration errors and manifold perturbations—within the covariance matrix structure and the array manifold model.
proposals.method: We will collect experimental data from a well-characterized, reconfigurable VHF/UHF antenna array in an anechoic chamber. The proposed WSF and WALS algorithms will be applied to this data, and their empirical error statistics will be compared to the theoretical Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) derived for a perfectly calibrated array. We will then incorporate structured covariance models (e.g., using a coupling matrix) and perturbed manifold models into the estimators to diagnose performance limits. The analysis will leverage the geometric framework of the array's covariance manifold to quantify sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, data-driven performance bounds for subspace-based estimators on physical arrays, directly linking theoretical covariance manifold properties to practical estimation error. It will yield concrete guidelines for the required calibration accuracy to achieve near-optimal performance with WSF/WALS in operational systems.
proposals.novelty: While WSF analysis is mature in simulation, the novelty lies in using controlled experimental data to validate and refine the mathematical models of array imperfections, creating a closed loop between the abstract manifold theory and physical sensor systems.

proposals.title: Robust Joint Array Calibration and DOA Estimation via Deterministic Annealed Variational EM with Subspace Initialization
proposals.motivation: Joint estimation of unknown array manifold perturbations and source directions-of-arrival (DOA) is critical for high-resolution sensor arrays in radar and communications, but remains challenging under low sample support or correlated noise. Traditional Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithms are highly sensitive to initialization and often converge to suboptimal local maxima, degrading performance in practical, non-ideal conditions. A robust, globally stable estimator is needed to ensure reliable performance in these scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Variational EM (VEM) framework, initialized by a robust subspace-based procedure and guided by a deterministic annealing schedule on its variational free energy, will yield a joint estimator with superior global convergence properties and more accurate uncertainty quantification compared to standard EM and decoupled methods.
proposals.method: The method first derives consistent initial estimates for gains and DOAs using an eigendecomposition of the sample covariance matrix, incorporating known calibration constraints to resolve ambiguities. The core estimation employs a VEM algorithm that approximates the full posterior distribution over the unknown gains, source waveforms, and DOAs, treating the latter as latent variables with a von Mises prior. A deterministic annealing schedule is applied by introducing a temperature parameter to the VEM's variational free energy, initially smoothing the likelihood landscape to avoid poor local traps before gradually cooling to the true posterior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, robust algorithm for joint array processing with proven convergence benefits from annealing. It will deliver a complete Bayesian treatment with practical uncertainty bounds for all estimated parameters, directly extending the Cramér-Rao Bound analyses in Friedlander's prior work. The resulting estimator will be validated against state-of-the-art methods for scenarios with low SNR, few snapshots, and correlated noise.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of deterministic annealing into a VEM framework for array processing, combined with a physically constrained subspace initialization, is a non-obvious synthesis that directly addresses the initialization and local optimum pitfalls of classical EM. This approach provides a principled Bayesian alternative to decoupled or alternating projection techniques prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Joint Array Calibration and Direction-of-Arrival Estimation via an EM Framework Inspired by Population Genetics Admixture Models
proposals.motivation: In array signal processing, joint estimation of unknown sensor gains, noise covariance, and source directions-of-arrival (DOA) is a critical but challenging problem, especially with incomplete array calibration data. Existing methods often rely on alternating optimization or subspace techniques that can be sensitive to initialization and model mismatches. A robust, statistically principled iterative framework is needed to handle this coupled estimation problem in the presence of noise and correlated sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm structure used for admixture inference in population genetics—which decomposes observed genotypes into latent ancestral proportions and allele frequencies—can be formally adapted to decompose observed array snapshots into latent source contributions, sensor gains, and noise parameters, leading to more stable and accurate joint estimation.
proposals.method: We will formulate the array observation model with unknown complex gains and noise covariance as a latent variable problem, where the latent variable is the probabilistic assignment of each sensor's measurement to underlying source directions. The E-step, analogous to estimating individual ancestry proportions, will compute the posterior probability of each source's contribution to each sensor output. The M-step, analogous to updating ancestral allele frequencies, will then update the maximum-likelihood estimates for the DOAs (parameterizing the 'source manifold'), the complex gain vector, and the noise covariance matrix, potentially leveraging manifold optimization techniques. The algorithm will be initialized using standard DOA estimation methods like MUSIC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, statistically rigorous EM algorithm for joint calibration and DOA estimation, directly extending the researcher's work on array manifolds and estimation bounds. It will offer a new algorithmic tool for handling array imperfections in practical settings, with potential applications in radar, communications, and acoustics. The formal analogy to genetics also creates a bridge for cross-disciplinary methodological exchange.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and mathematical mapping of the genetic admixture inference problem to the array processing joint estimation problem, which is non-obvious. While EM is used in signal processing, this specific formulation—treating sources as ancestral populations and sensor data as admixed observations—is a fresh perspective not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Joint Array Calibration and Direction-of-Arrival Estimation via Expectation-Maximization on the Covariance Manifold
proposals.motivation: Accurate direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation in sensor arrays is critically degraded by unknown or time-varying per-channel gain/phase errors and noise covariance. Pre-2026 methods often treat calibration as a separate, prior step or rely on simplified error models, limiting performance in practical, dynamic environments. A unified, statistically rigorous framework for joint estimation is needed to approach the fundamental bounds analyzed in Friedlander's prior work on the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound and array covariance manifolds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the joint estimation of DOAs, complex gains, and noise parameters as a maximum-likelihood problem on the structured covariance manifold, and solving it via a properly constrained Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, will yield estimates that asymptotically achieve the stochastic Cramér-Rao Bound for the complete parameter set.
proposals.method: The method will treat the unknown source signals as latent variables. The E-step will compute the conditional expectation of the complete-data log-likelihood, given the observations and current parameter estimates, leveraging the array signal model. The M-step will maximize this function with respect to all parameters: DOAs (via updates on the manifold of steering vectors), complex gains, and a structured noise covariance matrix. Critical innovations include enforcing the Hermitian Toeplitz structure of the calibrated array covariance during the M-step and developing efficient manifold optimization routines to update DOA estimates without grid search.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-loop, iterative algorithm for joint calibration and DOA estimation with proven convergence properties. It will deliver a practical tool that moves beyond the analysis of bounds to achieve them, directly impacting fields like radar, acoustics, and wireless communications. The theoretical analysis will deepen the understanding of the coupling between calibration errors and estimation performance on the array manifold.
proposals.novelty: While EM is known for DOA estimation, its application to the *joint* estimation problem on the structured covariance manifold—explicitly coupling gain, noise, and source parameters in a single likelihood framework—is novel. This moves beyond prior decoupled or alternating techniques by providing a unified statistical treatment grounded in the geometry of the array manifold.

proposals.title: Manifold Learning for Antenna Gain Surface Reconstruction: An Isomap Approach from Sparse Directional Measurements
proposals.motivation: Accurate characterization of antenna gain patterns across direction and frequency is critical for array calibration, direction-of-arrival estimation, and beamforming. Traditional methods require dense, anechoic chamber measurements, which are time-consuming and impractical for in-situ or wideband characterization. A method to reconstruct the complete gain manifold from sparse, empirical measurements would significantly accelerate antenna and array modeling and calibration.
proposals.hypothesis: The intrinsic structure of an antenna's complex gain, as a function of direction and frequency, lies on a low-dimensional, non-linear manifold. The Isomap algorithm can learn this manifold from sparse pairwise dissimilarities between gain measurements, enabling accurate reconstruction of the full gain surface.
proposals.method: We will formulate the antenna gain manifold by treating each measured complex gain vector (across frequency) at a specific direction as a point in a high-dimensional space. From a sparse set of such measurements, we will compute a pairwise distance matrix using a suitable metric (e.g., magnitude-phase distance). The Isomap algorithm will be applied to this matrix to estimate the intrinsic geodesic distances and embed the points into a low-dimensional Euclidean space. The resulting embedding will be used to train a kernel-based regressor (e.g., Gaussian Process) that maps from the low-dimensional coordinates back to the full gain vector, enabling interpolation to unmeasured directions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel data-driven framework for antenna gain modeling that reduces measurement burden. It will contribute a new perspective on the antenna gain manifold, linking it to concepts of array covariance manifolds and degrees of freedom. The method will be validated against ground-truth patterns for canonical and practical antenna elements.
proposals.novelty: While manifold learning is established in other fields, its application to reconstruct physical antenna gain surfaces from sparse data is novel. This adapts the core idea of Isomap—originally used for problems like protein folding from sparse distances—to a central problem in antenna array processing, creating a new bridge between computational geometry and electromagnetic characterization.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Federated Framework for Online Calibration of Dynamic Massive MIMO Arrays
proposals.motivation: Massive MIMO performance critically depends on accurate array calibration, but standard batch methods like Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) are ill-suited for dynamic environments where hardware impairments (gain/phase, mutual coupling) evolve over time. The latency of full-array batch processing leads to outdated calibration states, degrading beamforming gain and spectral efficiency in practical, non-stationary operating conditions. An online, scalable solution is needed to track these time-varying parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical calibration architecture, which separates fast-varying local impairments from slow-varying global parameters, can maintain calibration accuracy with low latency. This approach will outperform static or monolithic batch methods in dynamic scenarios by ensuring the calibration state closely tracks the true RF front-end state.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture. At the sub-array level, distributed Kalman filters will track fast-varying, local hardware impairments in real-time using pilot observations. At the central unit, a federated and sparsity-regularized WALS algorithm will operate asynchronously on aggregated sub-array data to estimate slow-varying global parameters (e.g., inter-sub-array coupling). Sparsity regularization, informed by the array's covariance manifold structure, will constrain the solution space for improved identifiability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, online calibration framework that maintains high array performance in dynamic conditions, directly addressing a key practical bottleneck for massive MIMO deployment. It will provide new theoretical insights into the separability and tracking of impairment parameters on the array manifold, extending prior work on degrees-of-freedom and covariance manifolds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled fusion of real-time distributed Kalman filtering with a federated, regularized batch algorithm for array calibration, explicitly designed to handle the multi-timescale nature of impairment dynamics. This hierarchical decomposition is a non-obvious extension of existing monolithic calibration paradigms.

proposals.title: A Recursive Weighted Alternating Least Squares Framework for Online Manifold Calibration of Dynamic Massive MIMO Arrays
proposals.motivation: Massive MIMO arrays in dynamic settings, such as UAV swarms or vehicle-to-everything networks, suffer from performance degradation due to time-varying impairments like element failure, platform vibration, and changing mutual coupling. Pre-deployment calibration is insufficient, and existing online methods are computationally prohibitive for arrays with hundreds of elements, creating a critical bottleneck for persistent, high-fidelity beamforming and sensing.
proposals.hypothesis: By exploiting the intrinsic low-dimensional structure and sparsity of large array covariance manifolds, a properly formulated recursive Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) algorithm can achieve scalable, communication-efficient online calibration with performance approaching the Cramér-Rao lower bound for dynamic parameter estimation.
proposals.method: We will develop a recursive WALS algorithm that decomposes the array manifold estimation into alternating updates for gain/phase parameters and source directions. The method will incorporate a forgetting factor for temporal weighting and leverage the sparse coupling structure of the array to localize updates, drastically reducing per-iteration complexity. The framework will be analytically linked to the manifold's degrees of freedom and validated via simulations of dynamic scenarios with moving sources and randomly failing elements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable, recursive calibration framework specifically designed for the dynamic impairments of massive arrays. It will deliver a concrete algorithm with proven convergence properties and a computational complexity analysis, enabling practical online calibration for next-generation reconfigurable intelligent surfaces and adaptive vehicular networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a recursive WALS formulation with a sparsity-aware update scheme tailored to the array's coupling structure, enabling true online operation for massive arrays—a significant advance over batch or block-adaptive methods dominant in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Manifold-Aware Algorithm Compression for High-Resolution Direction Finding on Miniaturized Sensor Arrays
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of small, embedded platforms (e.g., IoT nodes, micro-drones) demands high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation from severely resource-constrained arrays with few sensors. Pre-2026, classical subspace methods are computationally prohibitive for such platforms, while simpler methods sacrifice the resolution needed for dense signal environments. There is a critical gap in algorithms that are simultaneously computationally efficient, memory-light, and capable of approaching the fundamental performance bounds for small arrays.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly coupling the low-dimensional geometry of the array's covariance manifold with structured, low-precision numerical linear algebra, we can develop DOA estimators that achieve near-Cramér-Rao Bound performance for small arrays while requiring orders-of-magnitude fewer computational resources than standard subspace methods.
proposals.method: We will leverage the manifold structure of array covariance matrices, as explored in Friedlander's prior work, to design a compressed estimation pipeline. First, we will develop a quantized version of the sample covariance matrix that preserves manifold geometry. Second, we will derive a fast, approximate eigendecomposition for this structured matrix using randomized Krylov subspace methods tailored for embedded processors. Finally, we will integrate this with a manifold-aware grid search for spectral estimation, optimizing the coupling between the numerical approximation error and the resulting DOA estimation bias and variance.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a concrete, open-source algorithm suite for embedded DOA estimation, validated on low-power hardware. It will provide a new theoretical framework analyzing the trade-off between computational complexity and estimation accuracy specifically for small arrays, directly linking manifold geometry to implementable approximations.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct co-design of manifold-based statistical efficiency from array processing with resource-aware numerical linear algebra techniques from high-performance computing, an intersection not deeply explored in pre-2026 DOA literature for miniaturized systems.

proposals.title: A Dictionary-Based Global Optimization Framework for Wideband Radio Interferometric Calibration
proposals.motivation: Modern radio interferometers like the VLA and MeerKAT operate over wide bandwidths, but calibration is typically performed per narrowband channel, risking spectral structure loss and error propagation. Simultaneous multi-band calibration is crucial for high-dynamic-range imaging and robust spectral index estimation, yet remains a high-dimensional, non-convex inverse problem with strong coupling between gain, bandpass, and sky parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the MR Fingerprinting paradigm from medical MRI, a pseudo-randomized schedule of frequency observations, matched against a pre-computed dictionary of parameterized instrument and sky models, can provide a globally constrained solution for time-variable complex gains and frequency-dependent bandpass, intrinsically coupling the calibration across the entire bandwidth.
proposals.method: The method involves: 1) Designing a non-uniform sequence of frequency sub-band observations across the total bandwidth, analogous to an MR acquisition sequence. 2) Pre-computing a dictionary of simulated visibility vectors for a grid of parameters (gain trajectories, bandpass shapes, and compact source spectra) over the array manifold. 3) Formulating calibration as a global pattern-matching optimization, likely using a sparse reconstruction or nearest-neighbor search in the dictionary space, to jointly estimate all parameters by minimizing the mismatch between observed and dictionary visibilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physically constrained framework for wideband calibration that reduces spectral artifacts and improves solution robustness by exploiting the intrinsic coupling across frequencies. It will directly contribute to the theoretical understanding of the covariance manifold of an array under wideband, time-variable conditions.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the translation of the MR Fingerprinting dictionary-matching concept to radio interferometry, replacing traditional sequential or smoothness-prior-based calibration with a global, model-constrained pattern recognition approach for the coupled parameter set.

proposals.title: Robust Wideband Array Calibration via Spectral M-Estimators on the Covariance Manifold
proposals.motivation: Next-generation wideband radio telescopes like the Square Kilometre Array (SKA) require calibration across continuous bandwidths, which is highly sensitive to unmodeled sources and RFI. Traditional calibration assumes a perfect sky model, but outliers corrupt gain solutions and propagate errors into scientific data. A robust, data-driven method is urgently needed to ensure calibration quality in the presence of such spectral contaminants.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating robust M-estimators into a wideband cost function that respects the spectral smoothness of celestial sources will enable the joint estimation of antenna gains while automatically down-weighting the influence of spectrally erratic outliers, leading to more reliable calibration without a perfect prior model.
proposals.method: We will formulate calibration as an optimization on the array covariance manifold, extending Friedlander's manifold-based analysis. A wideband cost function will incorporate a spectral smoothness prior across frequency channels. Robust M-estimators (e.g., Huber, Tukey) will be applied to the residuals between observed and model visibilities, reweighting data points contaminated by RFI or bright unmodeled sources. We will develop an efficient iterative solver, likely a majorization-minimization approach, to handle the non-convex problem and estimate complex gains jointly across the band.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, robust calibration algorithm for wideband arrays that reduces dependency on perfect sky models. It will provide a theoretical framework for analyzing the robustness of calibration estimators on the covariance manifold and offer a path toward more automated calibration pipelines for SKA pathfinders and other instruments.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of robust M-estimation theory with wideband manifold optimization for calibration is novel. Unlike prior outlier-removal steps, this method integrates robustness directly into the spectral gain estimation, leveraging the intrinsic structure of the problem to distinguish celestial from interfering signals.

proposals.title: Manifold Geometry and Off-Grid Estimation for Arbitrary Sensor Arrays
proposals.motivation: Direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation for arbitrary, non-uniform, or non-linear arrays is critical for modern applications like autonomous vehicles and distributed MIMO, where sensor placement is constrained. Pre-2026, off-grid sparse recovery models, which address sources not aligned with a discrete dictionary, have been predominantly developed for uniform linear arrays (ULAs), leveraging their Vandermonde structure.
