Extending these powerful methods to arbitrary geometries is a significant open challenge, as the underlying manifold structure becomes more complex.
proposals.hypothesis: The intrinsic geometry of the array covariance manifold, characterized by its local curvature and degrees of freedom, can be exploited to formulate a principled, gridless estimation framework for arbitrary array configurations, leading to performance that approaches the deterministic Cramér-Rao bound.
proposals.method: We will extend the atomic norm minimization framework to arbitrary arrays by explicitly modeling the signal manifold as a continuous parameter space. The core technical approach involves deriving a semidefinite programming (SDP) formulation for the atomic norm that incorporates the specific array steering vectors. This will require analyzing the dual problem to establish optimality conditions and developing efficient solvers using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). Performance analysis will be conducted by linking the SDP's dual polynomial to the local curvature of the array manifold, drawing on Friedlander's prior work on covariance manifolds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, gridless DOA estimation algorithm applicable to any array geometry, removing the ULA restriction. It will theoretically characterize the resolution limits and estimation accuracy in terms of manifold curvature, offering new insights into the fundamental coupling between array geometry and estimation performance.
proposals.novelty: While off-grid methods for ULAs are established, a gridless, optimization-based framework for completely arbitrary arrays is novel. The key innovation is the integration of manifold geometry analysis from array processing with convex optimization techniques from compressed sensing to solve this generalized problem.

proposals.title: Performance Bounds and Manifold Geometry for Direction-of-Arrival Estimation with Correlated Sources
proposals.motivation: Most high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation algorithms, such as MUSIC and ESPRIT, rely on the assumption of uncorrelated source signals. In practical scenarios like multipath propagation or smart jamming, significant source correlation occurs, leading to severe performance degradation and rank-deficient signal covariance matrices. A rigorous, geometry-aware analysis of this performance breakdown is lacking, limiting the design of robust systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The degradation of DOA estimation performance under source correlation is fundamentally governed by the distortion of the signal subspace and the altered geometry of the array covariance manifold. This distortion can be quantified through a modified Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) analysis that explicitly accounts for signal correlation structure.
proposals.method: We will derive a closed-form, data-independent expression for the Stochastic CRB under arbitrary source correlation, extending Friedlander's prior work on CRB estimation. This theoretical bound will be analyzed in relation to the geometry of the array covariance manifold, examining how correlation affects identifiability and degrees of freedom. Performance evaluation will be conducted for subspace methods (MUSIC, ESPRIT) and sparse recovery techniques, using simulated data with controlled correlation levels and array configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework for understanding the fundamental limits of DOA estimation with correlated sources, linking information-theoretic bounds to manifold geometry. It will yield practical guidelines for array design and algorithm selection in correlated environments and propose mitigation strategies based on the geometric insights.
proposals.novelty: While source correlation is a known challenge, a unified analysis combining explicit CRB derivation, manifold geometry distortion, and a comparative study of subspace versus sparse methods under this condition is novel. This moves beyond empirical observations to a principled, geometric explanation of performance limits.

proposals.title: Off-Grid Sparse Bayesian Learning for Wideband Direction-of-Arrival Estimation via Frequency-Coupled Manifold Modeling
proposals.motivation: Modern radar, sonar, and communication systems increasingly rely on broadband signals for improved resolution and robustness. Existing high-resolution Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) methods, including off-grid sparse recovery techniques, are predominantly designed for narrowband signals, leading to performance degradation and model mismatch in wideband scenarios where the array manifold is frequency-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the frequency-dependent structure of the array manifold within a sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) framework, we can develop a unified off-grid estimation algorithm that coherently fuses multi-frequency data to achieve superior DOA accuracy and resolution for wideband sources.
proposals.method: We will extend the off-grid SBL model to a multi-frequency likelihood. A key innovation is the construction of a coupled, frequency-dependent manifold matrix, parameterized by a common set of source directions, enabling joint estimation across the band. The algorithm will employ an Expectation-Maximization (EM) procedure to iteratively estimate the source powers, noise variance, and the off-grid corrections, with a hierarchical prior promoting sparsity. Computational efficiency will be addressed via majorization-minimization techniques for the manifold coupling terms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, statistically principled algorithm for high-accuracy, off-grid wideband DOA estimation. It will yield new theoretical insights into the degrees of freedom and identifiability of the coupled wideband manifold, directly extending the researcher's prior work on covariance manifolds. The proposed method will be validated against state-of-the-art wideband MUSIC and focused covariance matrix techniques.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of an off-grid, continuous dictionary model into a coherent wideband SBL framework, explicitly addressing the manifold's frequency dispersion through a structured coupling model, which is a distinct departure from typical narrowband off-grid methods or incoherent wideband processing.

proposals.title: Robust Joint Estimation of Source Number and Directions-of-Arrival via Manifold-Aware Bayesian Model Selection
proposals.motivation: Accurate direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation in array processing fundamentally depends on knowing the number of sources, a parameter typically estimated in a separate, error-prone step. Existing information-theoretic criteria (e.g., AIC, MDL) are sensitive to model mismatches and low SNR, while subspace methods fail for coherent sources. A robust, joint estimation framework is needed to improve overall system reliability, particularly for applications like radar, sonar, and MIMO communications where model order errors cascade into DOA failures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the joint source number and DOA estimation as a Bayesian model selection problem over the coupled parameter-manifold space of the array, we can achieve superior robustness to noise, source correlation, and array imperfections compared to decoupled, sequential approaches.
proposals.method: The approach leverages the geometry of the array covariance manifold. We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where the number of sources is a discrete random variable with a prior. The likelihood will be defined on the complex Wishart distribution of the sample covariance matrix, conditioned on source parameters lying on the array manifold. Inference will be performed via a reversible-jump Markov Chain Monte Carlo (RJ-MCMC) sampler, which navigates between models of different dimensions, jointly sampling the source count, DOAs, and amplitudes. Calibration errors will be incorporated as nuisance parameters on the manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, statistically rigorous framework for joint model order and parameter estimation in array processing. It will yield a practical algorithm with quantifiable uncertainty bounds and directly extend the analysis of array degrees-of-freedom to finite-sample, mismatched scenarios. The results will offer a benchmark against which classical methods can be evaluated.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of Bayesian model selection with the differential geometry of the array covariance manifold within an RJ-MCMC sampler, moving beyond point estimates of information criteria to a full posterior distribution over the source count and parameters.

proposals.title: Optimal Signal Design for Frequency Estimation via Manifold Geometry and the Cramér-Rao Bound
proposals.motivation: In array processing and radar, frequency estimation accuracy is fundamentally limited by the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), which depends on both the array geometry and the transmitted signal waveform. While prior work by Friedlander and others has focused on analyzing the CRB for given signals and arrays, the inverse problem—designing the optimal signal waveform to minimize the CRB for a given array and scenario—remains underexplored. This is a critical gap for applications like cognitive radar and MIMO communications, where adaptive signal design can maximize estimation performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the parameter estimation problem on the covariance manifold of the array and treating the CRB as a function of the signal's time-frequency structure, we can formulate and solve a constrained optimization to design finite-energy signals that achieve the minimum possible frequency estimation error for a given array configuration.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage the mathematical framework of array covariance manifolds and CRB expressions for frequency/DOA estimation. We will formulate the signal design as an optimization problem: minimize the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the CRB matrix with respect to the signal's autocorrelation function or spectral density, subject to energy and bandwidth constraints. This will involve solving a semi-definite programming problem on the manifold of valid signal covariance matrices. The coupling between the signal's degrees of freedom and the array's manifold structure will be analyzed to derive necessary conditions for optimality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, closed-form methodology for optimal signal design tailored to specific array geometries, moving beyond ad-hoc waveform selection. It will yield new theoretical insights into the fundamental trade-offs between signal bandwidth, energy, and estimation accuracy, directly applicable to the calibration and operation of advanced antenna arrays.
proposals.novelty: While CRB analysis is standard, inverting it for active signal design is non-trivial and represents a shift from analysis to synthesis. The novelty lies in explicitly solving this inverse problem by combining CRB-based uncertainty quantification with optimization on the array's covariance manifold, a synthesis not fully addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Noise Covariance Estimation via Residual Manifold Analysis in Array Processing
proposals.motivation: In array signal processing, direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation accuracy is fundamentally bounded by the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), which depends critically on the unknown noise covariance structure. Existing methods often assume white noise or require separate calibration phases, limiting performance in practical, non-ideal environments. A method to directly estimate noise characteristics from the primary data residuals would enable more accurate CRB prediction and improved adaptive algorithms without dedicated calibration snapshots.
proposals.hypothesis: The residual vector, obtained after subtracting estimated signal components, lies on a structured covariance manifold determined by the array geometry and the true noise process. Analyzing the geometric and statistical properties of this residual can yield a consistent estimate of the noise covariance matrix.
proposals.method: We will formulate a dual optimization problem where, for a given signal subspace estimate, we minimize a distance metric between the sample covariance of the residuals and a structured noise covariance manifold. This manifold incorporates constraints like Toeplitz (for uniform linear arrays) or more general persymmetric structures. The approach leverages tools from Riemannian optimization on matrix manifolds, coupling the signal estimation error with the noise estimation error. Algorithms will be developed based on manifold gradient descent, with performance analyzed via the perturbation theory of sample covariance matrices on these constrained sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for joint signal and noise estimation, directly yielding refined CRB predictions as in Friedlander's prior work. It will contribute new algorithms for array calibration and enable more robust DOA estimation in scenarios with unknown, colored noise fields.
proposals.novelty: While residual analysis is common for model order selection, its systematic use for noise covariance estimation by exploiting the specific geometric structure (manifold) imposed by the array is unexplored. This inverts the typical problem, treating the noise not as a nuisance but as a structured entity to be estimated from the signal-subtracted data.

proposals.title: Exploiting Array Manifold Geometry and Low-Rank Structure for Real-Time Direction-of-Arrival Estimation
proposals.motivation: Real-time direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation is critical for modern applications like autonomous navigation, cognitive radio, and radar. However, high-resolution subspace methods like MUSIC and ESPRIT are computationally intensive, scaling poorly with array size and snapshot count. There is a pressing need for algorithms that maintain statistical efficiency while drastically reducing computational complexity to enable deployment on embedded platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly exploiting the intrinsic low-dimensional geometry of the array covariance manifold and the low-rank structure of the signal subspace, we can derive iterative estimation algorithms with near-Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRB) performance at a computational cost that scales linearly with the number of sensors and sources.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will employ a randomized sketching technique to rapidly compute a low-rank approximation of the sample covariance matrix, reducing the dominant O(M^2N) cost of covariance estimation for an M-element array with N snapshots. Second, we will formulate DOA estimation as an optimization on the array manifold, leveraging its known differential geometry (as studied in Friedlander's prior work on the covariance manifold) to design a Riemannian conjugate gradient or stochastic gradient descent solver. This avoids full eigenvalue decomposition, iteratively refining estimates from a coarse initial guess (e.g., from a root-MUSIC polynomial).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously derived, computationally efficient DOA estimation framework with a known performance-complexity trade-off, explicitly quantified relative to the CRB. It will deliver open-source algorithmic implementations suitable for real-time prototyping on software-defined radio platforms, bridging theoretical array processing and practical embedded system constraints.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of randomized linear algebra for covariance sketching with Riemannian optimization on the specific array manifold, moving beyond generic low-rank approximations to exploit the precise geometric structure governing the estimation problem.

proposals.title: Compressive Sensing for Sparse Polynomial-Phase Signal Estimation in Array Processing
proposals.motivation: Polynomial-phase signals (PPS) are fundamental models for radar, sonar, and communications, where parameters like Doppler rate are encoded in polynomial coefficients. Conventional estimation methods, like the high-order ambiguity function, require Nyquist-rate sampling, which is resource-intensive for wideband signals or large arrays. Compressive sensing (CS) offers a framework for sub-Nyquist sampling, but its direct application to PPS is challenged by the non-linear, non-sparse structure of the phase in the time domain.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a structured, overcomplete dictionary of discretized polynomial-phase bases and exploiting the intrinsic sparsity of the signal in the coefficient domain, the polynomial-phase coefficients can be accurately recovered from severely undersampled data using convex optimization, thereby breaking the Nyquist sampling barrier for this class of signals.
proposals.method: We will formulate the sparse PPS estimation as a basis pursuit denoising problem. A key innovation is the design of a manifold-aware dictionary where each atom corresponds to a specific vector of polynomial coefficients, mapping to the array's covariance manifold structure. The recovery algorithm will leverage `l1`-norm minimization (e.g., via SPGL1 or CVX) with constraints derived from the antenna array geometry. Performance bounds will be analyzed by deriving the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for the sparse estimation problem, extending Friedlander's prior work on DOA bounds to the compressive regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous CS-based sampling and estimation theory for PPS, directly reducing the required sampling rate and computational load for parameter estimation. It will extend the analysis of array covariance manifolds to the undersampled case and deliver a practical algorithm validated through simulations of radar-like scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While CS is established for linear models, its application to the inherently non-linear PPS parameter estimation via a structured dictionary tied to the array manifold is novel. The integration of manifold geometry and compressive parameter recovery for phase coefficients represents a significant departure from standard spectral CS techniques.

proposals.title: Manifold-Aware Discrete Polynomial-Phase Transform for Sparse and Non-Uniform Array Sampling
proposals.motivation: The Discrete Polynomial-Phase Transform (DPT) is a powerful tool for estimating parameters of polynomial-phase signals (PPS) in radar and communications, but it requires uniformly sampled data. Modern sparse and non-uniform arrays, crucial for reducing hardware costs and enabling flexible deployments, violate this assumption, degrading DPT performance. A method to adapt the DPT for such sampling scenarios is needed to bridge high-resolution parameter estimation with practical array architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the sampling-induced structure of the signal's covariance matrix on a Riemannian manifold, the DPT can be reformulated to operate directly on the manifold geometry, enabling accurate PPS parameter estimation from sparse or non-uniform samples.
proposals.method: We will leverage the geometric framework of array covariance manifolds, as explored in Friedlander's prior work. First, we will characterize the manifold of covariance matrices generated by PPS under arbitrary sampling sets. Second, we will develop a modified DPT algorithm that performs its higher-order lag products and multiplications along geodesics or tangent spaces of this manifold, rather than on a uniform time grid. This will involve coupling manifold optimization techniques (e.g., Riemannian gradient descent) with the core DPT structure to handle the missing data inherent in sparse sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the applicability of the DPT to modern sparse array systems, directly linking array manifold theory to non-stationary signal analysis. It will provide a new, theoretically grounded tool for direction-finding and waveform parameter estimation in scenarios with constrained or randomized sampling, impacting both algorithm design and system calibration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of DPT-based phase parameter estimation with the geometric analysis of array covariance manifolds under sparse sampling, a coupling not addressed in existing DPT or array processing literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Complex-Valued Ladder Estimation for Structured Covariance Matrices in Array Processing
proposals.motivation: In array signal processing for communications and radar, the sample covariance matrix of narrowband signals is often modeled as a complex-valued, Hermitian Toeplitz matrix. Efficient, high-resolution parameter estimation (e.g., for direction-of-arrival) relies on exploiting this structure. Pre-2026, the 'ladder' estimation framework provides a powerful, computationally efficient method for real-valued Toeplitz systems, but a direct, principled extension to the complex domain—essential for quadrature (I/Q) signal processing—is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the real-valued ladder estimation algorithm can be generalized to complex Hermitian Toeplitz systems by leveraging the geometry of complex manifolds and the coupling between subarray structures, yielding statistically efficient estimators with lower computational cost than unstructured maximum likelihood.
proposals.method: We will formulate the complex covariance matrix estimation as an optimization on the manifold of Hermitian positive-definite Toeplitz matrices. The core algorithm will extend the real-valued ladder's recursive structure by coupling the estimation of the first column (lags 0 to N-1) with its complex conjugate, respecting the Hermitian symmetry. We will derive the complex-valued gradient steps and Riemannian geometry (e.g., using the Wirtinger calculus) to ensure convergence. Performance will be benchmarked against the Cramér-Rao Bound derived for structured covariance models and standard methods like iterative quadratic maximum likelihood (IQML).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated complex-valued ladder algorithm, enabling fast, structure-aware covariance estimation for uniform linear arrays. It will contribute a new tool for array calibration and model-order selection by analyzing the degrees of freedom on the constrained covariance manifold. The results will directly improve preprocessing for high-resolution parameter estimation in coherent processing systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal geometric extension of the ladder concept to the complex field, moving beyond ad-hoc complexifications. It uniquely combines the recursive efficiency of the ladder with the manifold structure of complex Toeplitz matrices, a coupling not explicitly addressed in prior array processing literature.

proposals.title: Monte Carlo Characterization of Array Manifold Uncertainty and its Impact on Cramér-Rao Bounds for Direction-of-Arrival Estimation
proposals.motivation: Array processing performance bounds, such as the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), are typically derived assuming perfect knowledge of the array manifold. In practice, manifold uncertainties from calibration errors, mutual coupling, and element position perturbations are inevitable and degrade estimation performance. A rigorous, probabilistic framework is needed to quantify this degradation and inform robust array design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Monte Carlo simulation framework, sampling from probabilistic models of array imperfections, will reveal a statistically significant gap between the nominal CRB and the practically achievable estimation variance, and that this gap can be characterized by the geometry of the perturbed covariance manifold.
proposals.method: We will develop stochastic models for key uncertainty sources (e.g., Gaussian-distributed element position errors, complex Wishart-distributed coupling matrix perturbations). Using Monte Carlo methods, we will generate ensembles of perturbed manifold vectors and compute the corresponding sample covariance matrices and CRBs for given scenarios. The statistical distribution of the CRB will be analyzed, and its relationship to the underlying geometry of the perturbed manifold—extending concepts from the researcher's prior work on the covariance manifold—will be investigated to derive insightful metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, simulation-based tool for predicting realistic performance limits in uncertain array environments, complementing analytic CRB derivations. It will yield new insights into the sensitivity of different array geometries to specific imperfections, guiding the design of arrays and calibration procedures for robust performance.
proposals.novelty: While Monte Carlo is well-known, its systematic application to bridge the specific gap between the nominal array manifold, the perturbed covariance manifold geometry, and the statistical distribution of fundamental bounds like the CRB is novel. This integrates stochastic simulation directly into the core analytical framework of statistical array processing.

proposals.title: Robust Direction Finding via Total Least Squares on the Array Covariance Manifold
proposals.motivation: High-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation relies on an accurate array manifold, but in practice, this manifold is corrupted by sensor position errors, mutual coupling, and calibration imperfections. Standard least-squares methods, which assume errors only in the sensor outputs, yield biased estimates under these joint errors-in-variables conditions, degrading performance. A principled framework is needed to jointly mitigate the effects of errors in both the observed data and the array steering model.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating the DOA estimation problem within a structured Total Least Squares (TLS) framework that explicitly accounts for perturbations in both the sample covariance matrix and the array manifold will provide statistically consistent and lower-bias estimates compared to conventional estimators under realistic array imperfections.
proposals.method: We will develop a manifold-constrained TLS estimator. The method will model the perturbed sample covariance matrix and the perturbed array steering vectors jointly, seeking a low-rank signal subspace and corrected manifold parameters that best fit the data. The core algorithm will involve solving a structured TLS optimization problem on the manifold of Hermitian positive-semidefinite matrices, potentially using Riemannian optimization techniques. Performance will be analyzed by deriving the asymptotic estimation error and comparing it to the Cramér-Rao Bound for the joint error model, as established in Friedlander's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified estimation-theoretic framework for robust array processing in the presence of manifold uncertainties. It will deliver a practical, high-performance algorithm for DOA estimation under common hardware impairments and offer new theoretical insights into the coupling between data and model errors on estimation performance.
proposals.novelty: While TLS is known in numerical analysis, its application as a manifold-constrained estimator that jointly corrects the data covariance *and* the array steering model for DOA estimation is novel. This moves beyond standard errors-in-variables models by explicitly incorporating the geometric structure of the array covariance manifold.

proposals.title: Fundamental Limits and Calibration Manifold Geometry for Distributed Aperture Arrays
proposals.motivation: The push towards next-generation sensing and communication systems, such as 6G, terahertz imaging, and radio astronomy (e.g., the Square Kilometre Array), necessitates arrays with massive physical apertures or distributed elements. Pre-2026 theory, including Friedlander's work on the covariance manifold, struggles to characterize the fundamental estimation limits and calibration requirements when array dimensions become comparable to or exceed the spatial coherence scale of the wavefield, introducing significant non-idealities and mutual coupling variations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for very large aperture arrays, the intrinsic geometric structure of the calibration manifold imposes a fundamental trade-off between achievable degrees of freedom and calibration stability, and that this trade-off can be quantified via a manifold-intrinsic Cramér-Rao Bound analysis.
proposals.method: We will extend the covariance manifold framework to explicitly model spatially varying mutual coupling and phase errors across a distributed aperture. Using differential geometry, we will analyze the curvature and dimensionality of this high-dimensional manifold. The analysis will couple this geometric model with a derivation of the data-based Cramér-Rao Bound for direction-of-arrival estimation under these spatially correlated uncertainties. Numerical validation will employ synthetic arrays with thousands of elements, simulating near-field and far-field sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding the performance limits of distributed arrays, directly linking manifold geometry to estimation uncertainty. It will yield practical guidelines for the design of calibration sub-systems and array geometries that are robust to the scaling-induced uncertainties identified.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes arrays as points on a manifold, this proposal uniquely focuses on how the manifold's geometry itself evolves and constrains performance as the array scales to physically large dimensions, integrating coupling, calibration, and fundamental bounds into a single geometric analysis.

proposals.title: Fundamental Performance Limits of Compact Arrays: A Cramér-Rao Bound Analysis for Super-Resolution Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The drive towards miniaturized sensor platforms (e.g., for IoT, wearables, and UAVs) necessitates the use of compact antenna arrays with sub-wavelength element spacing. While super-resolution algorithms like MUSIC can theoretically resolve sources beyond the classical Rayleigh limit, their performance on such severely constrained arrays is poorly quantified. A rigorous, geometry-aware uncertainty analysis is needed to inform the design and fundamental capability limits of these compact systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) for direction-of-arrival estimation on compact arrays exhibits a distinct, quantifiable coupling between array manifold curvature and the achievable super-resolution threshold, fundamentally limiting performance even for high-SNR and ideal algorithms.
proposals.method: We will derive the exact CRB for a general compact array geometry, explicitly incorporating the structure of the array manifold and its coupling matrix. This bound will be evaluated for specific super-resolution algorithms, primarily MUSIC and its root variants, by analyzing the statistical efficiency of their estimates relative to the CRB. The analysis will focus on near-degenerate manifold scenarios characteristic of small apertures, using tools from differential geometry and statistical estimation theory as established in Friedlander's prior work on covariance manifolds and degrees of freedom.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form, predictive framework for the ultimate resolution limit of any compact array geometry, moving beyond empirical simulation. It will yield practical design guidelines for element placement in miniaturized systems and establish a benchmark for evaluating the efficiency of existing and future super-resolution techniques in the compact array regime.
proposals.novelty: While the CRB for standard arrays is well-known, its detailed analytical exploration for super-resolution algorithms applied to *compact* arrays with strong mutual coupling and manifold curvature is novel. This bridges Friedlander's work on the CRB and array manifold geometry with the practical challenge of miniaturization.

proposals.title: Self-Calibration via Uncertainty Manifold Inversion: A Data-Driven Approach to Array Error Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation in sensor arrays is critically degraded by unknown gain and phase errors. Traditional calibration requires precisely known sources or dedicated hardware, which is often impractical. A method for an array to autonomously estimate its own uncertainties using only operational signals would significantly enhance robustness in radar, communications, and acoustic sensing.
proposals.hypothesis: The intrinsic uncertainty in array manifold estimation, quantified by the Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) and the structure of the covariance manifold, can be inverted to directly estimate per-sensor gain and phase errors from observed data with a small number of known source directions.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a joint estimation on a product manifold of sensor errors and source parameters. Leveraging Friedlander's prior work on the covariance manifold and degrees of freedom, we will develop a maximum-likelihood estimator that treats the array uncertainties as the primary parameters, with known source directions as constraints. The algorithm will employ an iterative Riemannian optimization on the manifold of complex diagonal gain matrices, coupled with a subspace fitting criterion to the observed sample covariance matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, purely data-driven framework for array self-diagnosis and calibration, reducing dependency on external infrastructure. It will yield a closed-form connection between the CRLB for DOA estimation and the estimability of array errors, advancing the theory of uncertainty quantification in array processing.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard calibration that treats errors as a nuisance, this approach inverts the problem to make them the primary estimand. It uniquely combines the CRLB and manifold geometry to derive an estimator directly from the data's statistical uncertainty structure.

proposals.title: Cramér-Rao Bounds and Manifold Geometry for Direction-Finding with Broadband and Wideband Array Signals
proposals.motivation: Fundamental performance bounds, such as the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), for Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) estimation are predominantly derived under narrowband signal assumptions. Modern sensing and communications systems increasingly utilize wideband and broadband signals (e.g., radar, UWB, wideband communications), where frequency-dependent array responses and signal structure invalidate narrowband models. A rigorous, geometry-aware uncertainty analysis for these regimes is lacking but critical for system design and algorithm assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The intrinsic dimensionality and curvature of the broadband signal-plus-noise covariance manifold fundamentally alter the stochastic uncertainty bounds for parameter estimation, and these effects can be captured by a generalized CRB derived from a coupled, frequency-dependent array manifold model.
proposals.method: We will extend the manifold and CRB framework from Friedlander's prior work on narrowband arrays. First, we will formulate the parameterized covariance matrix for coherent and incoherent wideband signal models, treating the set of per-frequency steering vectors as a higher-order composite manifold. Using Slepian-Bangs formula and Riemannian geometry on this composite manifold, we will derive closed-form CRB expressions. The analysis will explicitly account for bandwidth, source spectrum, and array geometry coupling across frequency subbands, validated via numerical simulations with standard wideband algorithms like incoherent MUSIC and coherent frequency-domain focusing techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, manifold-based derivation of deterministic and stochastic CRBs for wideband DOA estimation, quantifying the explicit trade-off between bandwidth, array aperture, and estimation accuracy. It will yield practical design insights for wideband array systems and establish a theoretical benchmark for evaluating existing and future wideband estimation algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While narrowband CRB analysis is mature, a comprehensive geometric and information-theoretic treatment of wideband uncertainty, which couples the array manifold structure across a continuous frequency band, is novel. This moves beyond prior approximations or frequency-averaging techniques to a fundamental, coupled model.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Cramér-Rao Bound Analysis for Non-Radio Frequency Sensor Arrays: Application to Sonar and Seismic Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation in sonar, seismic, and acoustic microphone arrays is critically limited by array imperfections, such as uncertain sensor positions and coupling, especially in harsh or mobile deployment environments. Existing uncertainty analyses are often tailored to RF antenna arrays, leaving a gap in rigorous, data-driven performance bounds for these physically distinct sensor modalities where wave propagation models differ.
proposals.hypothesis: The data-based Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) estimation framework, developed for RF arrays, can be generalized to model the distinct covariance manifolds of sonar and seismic arrays, providing accurate, in-situ performance bounds despite unknown array perturbations and near-field or complex propagation effects.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mathematical framework from Friedlander's prior work on the array covariance manifold and data-based CRB estimation. The core approach involves modeling the received signal covariance matrix for acoustic/seismic arrays, incorporating specific manifold structures defined by sensor positions and medium-specific Green's functions. The method will be validated using simulated and real data from underwater sonar arrays (accounting for near-field and multipath) and seismic sensor networks, comparing the data-derived CRB to empirical estimator variance from algorithms like MUSIC and sparse reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, data-driven tool for assessing the fundamental estimation limits of non-RF sensor arrays under practical imperfections. It will yield new insights into the coupling between array geometry uncertainties, propagation physics, and estimation performance, directly informing the design and calibration of field-deployed sonar and seismic systems.
proposals.novelty: While data-based CRB analysis is established for RF, its application to the physically and geometrically distinct manifolds of sonar/seismic arrays, which operate in different media and often in the near-field, is novel and non-trivial, requiring a reformulation of the array manifold and uncertainty coupling models.

ideas.title: Recursive, Scalable Online Calibration for Dynamic Massive MIMO Arrays
ideas.description: This research develops a novel recursive Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) framework specifically designed for online calibration of massive MIMO arrays operating in dynamic environments. The core innovation is a computationally and communicationally efficient algorithm that continuously updates array manifold estimates as sources move, array elements degrade or are reconfigured, and the surrounding electromagnetic environment changes. By exploiting the inherent structure and sparsity of large arrays, the method achieves scalability to hundreds or thousands of elements while maintaining a lightweight recursive update, avoiding the prohibitive cost of full-batch recalibration. This enables persistent, high-fidelity beamforming and sensing for applications like dynamic UAV networks, adaptive vehicular communications, and next-generation reconfigurable intelligent surfaces.

ideas.title: Variational EM with Deterministic Annealing and Robust Subspace Initialization for Joint Array Parameter Estimation
ideas.description: This system first provides robust, consistent initial estimates for source directions and array gains using subspace decomposition and calibration constraints, mitigating EM's initialization sensitivity. The core estimation then uses a variational Expectation-Maximization (VEM) algorithm that approximates the full posterior over gains, noise, and directions, maintaining uncertainty measures. A deterministic annealing schedule is applied to the VEM's free energy, gradually introducing the likelihood's influence to avoid early convergence to poor local maxima. This results in a globally more stable and reliable joint estimator, particularly under challenging conditions of correlated noise or low sample support.

ideas.title: Validate WALS/WSF with Physical Sensor Data via Geophysical Array Calibration Methods
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to validate the proposed WALS and WSF array processing methods by adapting a solution from geophysics. In seismology, theoretical signal processing algorithms for sensor arrays are rigorously tested using data from permanent, calibrated seismic monitoring arrays where source events are independently verified. The analogous solution is to deploy the new algorithms on experimental data from physical sensor arrays (e.g., acoustic, RF, or other wave-based systems) with known ground truth. The adapted research idea involves sourcing datasets from existing experimental array installations where the true signal parameters (like direction-of-arrival) are known, then applying WALS and WSF to this real-world data. Performance would be benchmarked against both the ground truth and established methods, assessing accuracy, resolution limits, and practical robustness in the presence of calibration errors, non-ideal array geometries, and complex noise environments inherent to physical systems.

ideas.title: Calibration via Coupling-Aware Network Tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from network tomography, a field in network science used to infer internal link states from end-to-end path measurements in systems like the internet. The analogous problem is inferring hidden network parameters from aggregate observations. Here, the sensor array is conceptualized as a network where mutual electromagnetic couplings act as links between element nodes. The received signal at each sensor is a 'path measurement' combining the direct external source contribution and the signals coupled from all other sensors. By framing array calibration as a network tomography problem, we develop algorithms to solve the joint inverse problem—estimating both the external scene (source calibration) and the internal coupling matrix—using constrained optimization techniques that leverage the typically sparse or structured nature of the electromagnetic coupling network. This moves beyond simple isolation/de-embedding and actively uses the coupling as an informative signal for system identification.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Online Calibration for Dynamic Massive MIMO Arrays
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical, online calibration framework for massive MIMO arrays that overcomes the latency and dynamic tracking limitations of batch algorithms like WALS. The system employs a two-tier architecture: distributed Kalman filters at the sub-array level provide real-time tracking of fast-varying hardware impairments, while a federated and sparsity-regularized WALS algorithm operates asynchronously on a central unit to calibrate slow-varying global parameters. This design ensures calibration remains current with the actual RF front-end state under practical operating conditions. The result is a robust, scalable calibration method that maintains high beamforming gain and spectral efficiency in dynamically changing environments.

ideas.title: Apply EM for Joint Estimation via Population Genetics Admixture Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Expectation-Maximization (EM) framework from population genetics admixture inference to solve a joint estimation problem in array signal processing. In genetics, EM estimates latent ancestry proportions and ancestral allele frequencies from genotype data. Analogously, here we treat mixed sensor array data as observations from 'admixed' sources. The algorithm will perform an E-step to estimate the probabilistic contribution (gain) of each source direction to each sensor measurement, treating source presence as a latent variable. The M-step will then update the maximum-likelihood estimates for the source directions (like ancestral populations) and the noise covariance parameters. This enables robust iterative joint estimation of gains, noise, and source directions from incomplete or noisy multichannel observations, mirroring the structure of genetic ancestry decomposition.

ideas.title: Adaptive Blind Source Separation for Partially Correlated Calibrators
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption in radio interferometric calibration that calibrator sources are fully uncorrelated. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where Independent Component Analysis (ICA) algorithms were modified to handle partially dependent neural sources, we propose adapting 'correlation-aware' source separation techniques. In neuroscience, solutions involved modifying contrast functions or using multi-stage approaches that incorporate prior knowledge of source dependencies. Adapted to calibration, this would mean developing a new algorithmic framework that jointly estimates antenna gains and the partial correlation structure between calibrator signals. The solution might involve a regularized maximum likelihood approach where the penalty term encodes the expected deviation from full independence, or a hierarchical model that first identifies a subspace of correlated emission before performing gain estimation within that constrained model. This would improve calibration accuracy for observations of complex, extended, or partially coherent calibrator fields.

ideas.title: Use Isomap to model antenna gain manifolds from sparse measurements
ideas.description: This idea adapts a manifold learning solution from computational biology—specifically the use of Isomap for protein folding from sparse distance data—to the problem of modeling antenna gain patterns. The analogous solution reconstructs a non-linear 3D shape from incomplete pairwise distances. Adapted here, we treat sparse, multi-parameter antenna gain measurements as points on a low-dimensional manifold embedded in a high-dimensional space of directions and frequencies. By applying Isomap, we can model the complete directional gain pattern by preserving the intrinsic geodesic distances on this manifold, enabling accurate reconstruction and interpolation of the gain surface from limited empirical data.

ideas.title: Joint Robust Estimation of Calibration Parameters and Unknown Source Correlation Structure
ideas.description: This approach develops a unified framework that does not require prior knowledge of the correlational structure between calibrator sources. It treats the correlation matrix as an unknown, high-dimensional parameter to be inferred jointly with the primary calibration gains. The method uses a probabilistic model with regularization to avoid overfitting and incorporates model selection to distinguish between physical correlation and systematics. This results in a self-calibration algorithm that is robust to partially correlated sources and provides quantified uncertainties for both the calibration solutions and the inferred correlation structure itself.

ideas.title: Multi-Band Calibration via Spectral Self-Calibration
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of jointly calibrating multiple frequency bands by drawing an analogy from medical MRI, specifically the MR Fingerprinting technique. In that field, a pseudo-randomized acquisition sequence is matched against a pre-computed dictionary of physical signal models to simultaneously estimate multiple tissue parameters. Adapted to radio interferometry, the calibration framework would use a designed, non-uniform sequence of frequency band observations across the total bandwidth. The correlated visibilities would then be compared to a dictionary of simulated measurements generated from a parameterized sky and instrument model. A global pattern-matching optimization would simultaneously solve for the time-variable complex gains, the frequency-dependent bandpass response, and possibly the spectral indices of compact sources, thereby intrinsically coupling and constraining the calibration solutions across all observed frequencies within the wide bandwidth.

ideas.title: Robust Wideband Calibration via Spectral M-Estimators
ideas.description: This research develops a novel calibration framework that jointly solves for antenna gains across a wide, continuous bandwidth while inherently mitigating the influence of outlier sources. By integrating M-estimators into a wideband cost function, the method leverages the expected smooth spectral structure of true celestial sources to distinguish them from spectrally erratic outliers or interference. This allows for robust calibration directly from the data, eliminating the need for a perfectly clean sky model. The result is a more reliable and automated calibration pipeline for next-generation wideband radio telescopes, improving data quality in the presence of unforeseen RFI or bright, unmodeled sources.

ideas.title: Spectrally-constrained, direction-dependent wideband calibration using degenerate model separation
ideas.description: This research designs a calibration framework for wideband interferometric data that directly addresses the ill-posedness caused by the degeneracy between source spectrum and instrumental response. The approach imposes physically motivated spectral constraints on both the sky model (e.g., via spectral index maps) and the antenna gains (e.g., smoothness in frequency) within a unified optimization. By solving for these separable components simultaneously with appropriate regularization, the system enables robust wideband calibration, suppresses spurious spectral structure in solutions, and produces more accurate wideband sky images and source spectra.

ideas.title: Low-Precision Quantized WALS for Scalable and Energy-Efficient Massive MIMO Calibration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel adaptation of the Weighted Alternating Least Squares (WALS) algorithm for massive MIMO calibration, where all matrix operations are performed using low-precision, quantized numerical representations (e.g., INT8, FP16). By applying quantization-aware training principles to the calibration optimization loop, the algorithm significantly accelerates each WALS iteration and reduces memory bandwidth pressure. This allows the calibration process to scale to thousands of antenna elements while maintaining robustness to quantization noise through tailored gradient scaling and rounding-aware updates. The result is a scalable, energy-efficient calibration framework suitable for next-generation communication systems where traditional high-precision methods become prohibitively expensive.

ideas.title: Distributed Real-Time Calibration for Unknown Moving Sources
ideas.description: This research fuses the need to calibrate with moving sources of unknown trajectory with a distributed, multi-GPU architecture to create a system capable of real-time calibration for next-generation radio telescopes. The core innovation is a distributed optimization framework that partitions the high-dimensional search space—splitting trajectory parameters, time-varying gains, and sky model updates—across multiple nodes or GPUs, coordinating through a consensus mechanism. This allows the system to continuously ingest data from a vast array and jointly solve for the instrument's complex gains and the calibrators' dynamic paths without a stationary source assumption. The result is a scalable calibration pipeline that maintains data fidelity for high-cadence observations in the presence of aircraft, satellites, or natural moving sources, enabling new observational modes.

ideas.title: Apply Robust Geodetic Estimation to Source Localization
ideas.description: This idea adapts robust estimation techniques from geodesy and surveying, where they are used to compute stable reference frames from networks of measurements containing outliers (e.g., faulty GPS signals). The analogous solution involves iterative re-weighted least squares with M-estimators (like Huber or Tukey) to down-weight outlier measurements during network adjustment. Adapted to the original context of handling outlier sources, this means implementing a robust iterative estimation algorithm for source localization or intensity recovery. The algorithm will calculate residuals for each sensor's contribution to the estimated source parameters, apply a robust weighting function to down-weight sensors with large residuals (likely affected by outlier sources), and re-estimate until convergence, thereby producing source estimates that are resilient to corrupt or anomalous source signals in the data.

ideas.title: On-Device Blind Calibration for Miniature Sensor Arrays
ideas.description: This research fuses miniaturization for embedded arrays with blind source separation to create a novel framework for self-calibrating, ultra-small sensor nodes. It develops and optimizes lightweight, computationally frugal Independent Component Analysis (ICA) variants specifically for microcontrollers with few sensors, enabling them to separate superimposed calibration source signals from operational interference in real-time. This allows for accurate calibration to be performed autonomously in the field without controlled conditions or manual intervention, dramatically improving the long-term reliability and data fidelity of miniature IoT and wearable sensing systems.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Deconvolution for Calibrator Separation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where blind source separation techniques like deconvolution are used to disentangle superimposed seismic signals from multiple subsurface reflections and noise. The analogous problem is solved by exploiting statistical independence and sparsity of the source wavelets. Adapted to the calibration context, we employ ICA, but with constraints informed by the known approximate properties (e.g., frequency band, temporal structure) of the calibrator signal, to separate it from internal system noise and external interference before the calibration algorithm is applied, thereby improving calibration accuracy in noisy, multi-source environments.

ideas.title: Simultaneous Localization and Calibration for Moving Astronomical Sources
ideas.description: This idea challenges the stationary source assumption in radio interferometric calibration by drawing an analogy from robotic SLAM. In SLAM, a robot simultaneously estimates its own motion and a map of its surroundings. Adapted here, the moving calibrator source (like a satellite) is analogous to the robot, and the telescope's time-variable gain parameters are analogous to the map. The solution involves developing a 'Simultaneous Localization and Calibration' framework that uses the interferometric visibilities to jointly solve for the source's kinematic trajectory and the instrumental gains, potentially using techniques like factor graphs or expectation-maximization to handle the coupled unknowns, thereby enabling calibration with non-stationary celestial or artificial sources.

ideas.title: Robust Privacy Analysis for Adaptive Beamforming Under Adversarial Data on Learned Manifolds
ideas.description: This research investigates the privacy guarantees of adaptive beamforming algorithms when their directional gain pattern models are learned and updated via nonlinear manifold learning techniques, such as Isomap or Laplacian Eigenmaps. The core challenge is to analyze how adversarial or highly correlated sequential data updates—which can strategically distort the learned low-dimensional manifold structure—compromise differential privacy or other privacy metrics. This creates a novel analysis framework that moves beyond i.i.d. assumptions to account for the complex geometry of signal spaces and intentional poisoning attacks. The outcome is a set of robust privacy bounds and mitigation strategies for next-generation, geometry-aware wireless systems operating in non-stationary and potentially hostile environments.

ideas.title: Calibrating Refraction Models via a Geophysical Data Assimilation Framework
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of calibrating specific atmospheric or ionospheric refractive propagation models by drawing an analogy from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP). In NWP, data assimilation techniques are used to ingest diverse observational data to correct and refine imperfect forecast models. The analogous solution is adapted by treating the Wave Structure Function (WSF), calculated from measured signal propagation data (e.g., from satellite links or radar), as the critical observational dataset. The extended method would implement a data assimilation framework where the parameters of a defined refraction model are iteratively adjusted to minimize the difference between the model-predicted WSF and the empirically derived WSF. This process would calibrate the refraction model for specific temporal and spatial conditions, enhancing its accuracy for applications in radio communications, radar, and astronomy.

ideas.description: This research investigates the convergence behavior of the Wavefront Sensing and Control (WSF) calibration method when extended to more than two iterations, specifically within the context of defined, complex atmospheric or ionospheric refractive models. It will analyze how the non-linearities and spatial variations inherent in these realistic propagation environments impact the algorithm's convergence rate, stability, and final solution accuracy across multiple iterations. The study aims to identify conditions under which additional iterations lead to meaningful performance gains versus scenarios of diminishing returns or divergence, thereby providing guidelines for optimally configuring iterative calibration for specific operational environments. This creates a novel framework for evaluating the practical feasibility and resource requirements of high-precision calibration in adaptive optics and radio astronomy under turbulent conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Compressed Sensing's Random Projection for Fast, Non-Bayesian DP Optimization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that efficient differentially private (DP) methods must be intrinsically linked to Bayesian posterior sampling. Drawing an analogy from compressed sensing in signal processing—which recovers sparse signals from random, sub-Nyquist projections via convex optimization—we propose a new DP optimization framework. The analogous solution uses random linear projections (e.g., via a random matrix) for dimensionality reduction and noise shaping, followed by deterministic L1-minimization. Adapted to the DP context, we design a method where private data or stochastic gradients are first projected onto a random, lower-dimensional space where calibrated Gaussian noise is added efficiently. A subsequent deterministic, sparsity-aware optimization algorithm (e.g., proximal gradient descent on a perturbed loss) reconstructs the final model. This approach aims to provide DP guarantees with speed comparable to non-private SGD, entirely bypassing the need for any Bayesian posterior sampling mechanism.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes to test the WALS and WSF algorithms using real-world experimental data from physical sensor arrays, but with a crucial twist: the analysis and experiments will be explicitly designed to model and inject adversarial or highly correlated sequential data updates. The synergy lies in using the empirical array data to construct realistic threat models for correlation and adversarial influence, then rigorously measuring the deviation from theoretical i.i.d. privacy guarantees. This will produce the first benchmark of practical privacy resilience for these methods against the non-ideal, structured data streams found in actual deployments, bridging the gap between theoretical adversarial analysis and physical system validation.

ideas.title: Spectrally Segmented and Robust Multi-Band Calibration
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond a single joint fit by first performing a spectral classification of the data to identify distinct regimes like smooth continuum, spectral lines, and corrupted channels. Each regime is calibrated with an appropriately constrained model, preventing corruption from propagating across the entire band. A final global optimization step combines these solutions using robust weighting based on each segment's internal consistency, yielding a precise wideband calibration that is resilient to real-world instrumental and astrophysical spectral complexities.

ideas.title: Calibrating for Broadband Signals via Multi-Scale Decomposition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the narrowband signal model in radio astronomy calibration by drawing an analogy from seismic imaging in geophysics. There, the analogous problem of imaging complex geology with broadband seismic waves is solved using multi-scale decomposition (e.g., wavelet transforms) to handle frequency-dependent propagation effects per sub-band before reintegration. Adapted to the research context, the solution is to extend the calibration framework by first decomposing wideband astronomical signals into multiple sub-bands via a wavelet packet transform. Calibration parameters (e.g., direction-independent and dependent gains) are then solved for each sub-band, explicitly modeling chromatic effects. The final step synthesizes these solutions, using constraints across bands to produce a robust, broadband calibration and a spectrally consistent sky model, moving beyond the limiting narrowband assumption.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Multi-Source Calibration for Unknown Trajectories
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses the inherent degeneracy by embedding a differentiable, physics-constrained motion model (e.g., for satellites or aircraft) directly into the calibration optimization loop, providing a strong prior on plausible trajectories. It simultaneously observes multiple moving calibrator sources with distinct, unknown paths, using their collective geometric and temporal signatures to disentangle instrument gains from source parameters. The system employs a hierarchical Bayesian framework to jointly infer instrument calibration, source trajectories, and the hyperparameters of the motion model, quantifying all uncertainties. This results in a robust, self-calibrating system that does not require precise a priori knowledge of the calibrators' motions.

ideas.title: Private Posterior Sampling on Learned Manifolds for High-Dimensional Sensitive Data
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a two-stage framework for private learning on high-dimensional, structured data. First, a public, non-sensitive dataset (e.g., general antenna designs or public medical images) is used to learn a low-dimensional manifold via nonlinear dimensionality reduction, capturing the essential geometric constraints of the data domain. Second, for a new sensitive dataset (e.g., a company's proprietary antenna measurements or a hospital's patient scans), the SGLD-based private posterior sampling algorithm is applied strictly within the coordinates of this pre-learned manifold. This confines the noisy, privacy-preserving updates to the intrinsically relevant low-dimensional space, drastically improving utility and convergence for the private learner compared to applying SGLD directly in the raw, high-dimensional space.

ideas.description: This system dynamically diagnoses the level and structure of contamination in source directions or intensities to select an optimal robust statistical strategy. It evaluates multiple robust estimators against stability metrics and agreement across bootstrapped data subsamples to infer the contamination profile. The final estimation is performed using the adaptively chosen method or a weighted combination, ensuring robustness across unknown and mixed outlier types without requiring pre-specified tuning parameters.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Processing's SIMD Waveform Correlation for Parallelized Stochastic Gradient Noise
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysical signal processing, where SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) CPU intrinsics like AVX are used to parallelize the cross-correlation of seismic waveform arrays, a core but expensive operation. The analogous solution involves restructuring contiguous data blocks to fit vector registers and performing identical arithmetic operations across all elements simultaneously. Adapted to the machine learning context, this approach would restructure parameter gradient vectors or blocks into aligned memory buffers. Hardware-specific intrinsics would then be employed to execute vectorized random number generation and noise addition operations, parallelizing the stochastic steps of gradient descent across many parameters at once. This transforms a traditionally sequential sampling process into a batched, parallel computation, potentially yielding significant speedups in training algorithms that rely on stochastic gradient noise, such as in differential privacy or stochastic optimization.

ideas.title: Resource-Aware Adaptive Private Bayesian Learning for Embedded Sensor Arrays
ideas.description: This research fuses adaptive SGLD with embedded systems constraints to develop a novel, resource-aware Bayesian learning algorithm for small sensor arrays. The core innovation is an adaptive optimizer whose preconditioning matrix is co-designed with model sparsity and fixed-point arithmetic constraints, dynamically trading off precision for lower memory and energy use per iteration. This enables efficient, differentially private posterior sampling on-device, allowing each sensor node to perform local uncertainty quantification and model personalization without raw data transmission. The synergy lies in using the adaptive mechanism not only to accelerate convergence but to actively govern hardware resource consumption, making principled private Bayesian learning feasible for the first time on severely resource-limited platforms.

It first develops a high-fidelity forward model to synthetically impose a wide spectrum of realistic hardware and environmental imperfections onto controlled simulated data, enabling systematic stress-testing and sensitivity analysis. The core validation then proceeds against a curated, open benchmark suite of experimental array datasets spanning different sensor technologies (acoustic, RF, seismic) and source scenarios. Finally, performance is quantified using robust statistical measures that explicitly separate in-sample calibration error from out-of-sample prediction error across the diverse dataset collection, ensuring reported robustness is generalizable.

ideas.title: Seismic Inversion for Private Model Data Leakage
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of seismic inversion from geophysics to the problem of data reconstruction from private models. In seismology, the interior structure of the Earth is inferred from surface measurements of seismic waves; here, the 'subsurface' is the private training data, and the 'surface measurements' are the observable characteristics of the released model (its weights, gradients on specific queries, or membership inference scores). The analogous solution involves building a forward model of the training process (or a simplified proxy), then running an iterative inversion algorithm. Starting from a random or prior-based guess of the training data, the algorithm would adjust this guess to produce a synthetic model whose properties best match the actual released private model. This provides a systematic, optimization-based framework to quantify and bound what user data can be statistically or exactly reconstructed, analyzing the fundamental privacy leakage as a function of model scale, architecture, and the strength of the privacy guarantee applied during training.

The core innovation is an ultra-lightweight online learning module that continuously profiles the incoming data's access patterns and dynamically adjusts a compact, parameterized caching policy without prior distribution assumptions. This enables efficient real-time data prioritization and prediction directly on the device, minimizing off-chip communication and extending operational life, even when sensor data patterns shift unexpectedly due to environmental changes or task reconfiguration.

ideas.title: Dynamic Privacy-Preserving Calibration for Non-Stationary Data Streams
ideas.description: This research direction develops a method to dynamically calibrate the privacy parameters of SGLD-based algorithms when training on data from a source with an unknown, non-stationary distribution (e.g., a moving sensor or evolving user behavior). Instead of a fixed performance target, the system maintains a moving target for model utility (accuracy/throughput) as the data distribution drifts. The core innovation is an online optimization that continuously adjusts the SGLD noise injection to maximize the instantaneous differential privacy guarantee, conditioned on meeting the current, adaptive utility constraint imposed by the drifting data source. This enables robust, privacy-maximizing learning in environments where both the data and the optimal model are in flux.

ideas.title: Adaptive Precision SGLD via Signal Processing Quantization Techniques
ideas.description: This idea expands on low-precision/quantized training for SGLD by drawing an analogy from Digital Signal Processing. In DSP, adaptive quantization techniques like μ-law companding are used to non-uniformly map signal amplitudes to digital values, preserving detail in frequent, low-amplitude signals while coarsely representing high amplitudes. The analogous solution is to apply this amplitude-dependent precision allocation to the optimization process. The adapted research idea is to design a 'gradient compander' for SGLD training. This module would apply a non-linear transformation to gradient or parameter update magnitudes before quantization, ensuring high precision for small updates (crucial for the noise-informed exploration of Langevin dynamics) and lower precision for larger steps. This approach aims to accelerate SGLD steps and recommendation generation more effectively than uniform quantization by better preserving the statistical properties of the sampling process within a constrained numerical representation.

ideas.description: This research investigates the necessity of adaptive algorithm complexity for maintaining high accuracy in differentially private online learning systems. It develops a meta-algorithm that recursively calibrates not only its statistical outputs but also the structural complexity of its core private estimation mechanism in response to detected shifts in data sources or utility feedback. The core challenge is to design the complexity adaptation rule itself to be private and efficient, creating a closed-loop system where privacy constraints dynamically shape algorithmic sophistication. This enables intelligent allocation of the privacy budget across both computation and statistical estimation to optimize long-term performance in non-stationary environments.

This creates a closed-loop system where attack success metrics directly inform the design of the primary algorithm, leading to new DP mechanisms whose privacy guarantees are validated against strong, realistic inference threats rather than just theoretical composition bounds.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Privacy-Utility Frontier via Optimal Data Reconstruction Attacks
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to analyze and improve the privacy-accuracy frontier of a private algorithm by directly quantifying the privacy leakage via the feasibility of inverting the mechanism to reconstruct training data. Instead of using generic differential privacy parameters, the utility of the model is explicitly traded off against the success rate and fidelity of a theoretically optimal reconstruction attack. This enables the development of mechanisms that are provably optimal against a concrete, strong adversarial inference goal, and allows for the derivation of tighter, instance-specific trade-off curves grounded in a realistic threat model.

ideas.title: Fault-Aware Recursive Calibration with Meta-Detection for Abrupt Regime Shifts
ideas.description: This system enhances online calibration for dynamic environments with a dual-layer architecture. The primary layer performs recursive calibration, while a parallel meta-calibration layer continuously monitors for anomalies and evidence of abrupt regime shifts, such as sensor failures or new interference sources. Upon detection, the system automatically triggers a safeguarded recovery protocol, which may include model reselection, data purification, or a controlled reset using robust historical anchors. This approach maintains continuous calibration availability and prevents catastrophic divergence without requiring manual intervention.

ideas.title: Adapting Robust Control Theory for Privacy Analysis Under Non-I.I.D. Data Streams
ideas.description: This research adapts solution concepts from Robust Control Theory to analyze algorithmic privacy guarantees under adversarial or correlated sequential data updates. In control engineering, robust control methods (like H-infinity and μ-synthesis) mathematically guarantee system stability and performance despite bounded disturbances, model uncertainties, and correlated input patterns—overcoming the limitation of assuming stationary, independent inputs. By analogy, we can model the sequential data updates in a differentially private algorithm as a 'disturbance signal' with bounded magnitude and known correlation structure. The adapted research idea develops new privacy accounting frameworks that provide rigorous, composable privacy bounds under these non-i.i.d. conditions, similar to how robust controllers provide stability certificates. This involves defining appropriate 'norms' or measures for the adversarial update patterns and deriving composition theorems that guarantee privacy even when updates are strategically correlated or deliberately chosen to maximize privacy loss.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Algorithmic Complexity Against Fundamental Privacy-Accuracy Limits
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous framework to determine if complex algorithms are necessary by first deriving fundamental information-theoretic lower bounds on accuracy under strict privacy constraints for given problem classes. It then conducts a systematic empirical and theoretical audit, comparing both simple and complex state-of-the-art algorithms against these established limits. The outcome is a principled taxonomy identifying problem regimes where complexity is justified (because simple algorithms are provably far from optimal) versus where it is superfluous (because both simple and complex algorithms hit the same fundamental barrier). This moves the field beyond heuristic arguments for complexity towards a limit-aware theory of algorithmic design.

ideas.title: Online Calibration via Homeostatic Control Loops
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of online calibration for dynamic environments by drawing an analogy from systems physiology, specifically the biological principle of homeostasis. In living organisms, homeostatic control loops (e.g., for body temperature or blood pH) use continuous sensor feedback, recursive error computation, and effector adjustments to maintain stability despite external perturbations. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing the calibration system as a homeostatic regulator for a sensor array or data pipeline. The adapted idea proposes developing a recursive calibration framework with three core components mirroring a biological control loop: 1) A calibration state estimator that continuously monitors performance metrics (sensor), 2) A model-based controller that recursively computes optimal parameter adjustments based on deviation from a target accuracy (controller), and 3) An update mechanism that applies corrections to the live system (effector). This creates a closed-loop, continuous calibration process that autonomously maintains accuracy as sources change, array configurations drift, or environmental conditions evolve, effectively giving the system 'homeostatic' calibration properties.

ideas.title: Mechanism Synthesis for Provable Reconstruction Hardness in Private Model Release
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to not just analyze, but actively prevent, data reconstruction from private models. It formalizes the inverse problem as an adversarial game, deriving theoretical upper bounds on the accuracy of any possible reconstruction attack for a given model training algorithm. These bounds are then used as constraints within an optimization loop to synthesize new model release mechanisms or to select hyperparameters for existing ones (like DP-SGD) that minimize the worst-case reconstructability of training points. The outcome is a principled design methodology for creating models that are private by construction against reconstruction adversaries, moving beyond passive leakage assessment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Iteration-Computation Trade-off Frameworks for Multi-Round Convergent Algorithms
ideas.description: This research develops a principled methodology for analyzing and optimizing algorithms that inherently require many iterations to converge, focusing on domains like federated learning or large-scale non-convex optimization. It moves beyond simple performance profiling by constructing a formal cost model that balances per-iteration computational expense against the total number of iterations needed. The core output is a family of adaptive strategies that dynamically adjust algorithmic hyperparameters or computational effort per round based on convergence diagnostics, provably minimizing total resource consumption for a target solution quality. This provides a general, actionable framework for accelerating slow-converging processes in data-intensive computing environments.
